* The word ''crap'' did not originate as a [[back-formation]] of British plumber [[Thomas Crapper]]'s [[aptronym|apt surname]].<ref name="Harper 2010">{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Harper |first=Douglas |date=2010 |title=Crap |url=http://www.etymonline.com/index.php?term=crap |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140427083257/http://www.etymonline.com/index.php?term=crap |archive-date=April 27, 2014 |access-date=July 7, 2024 |work=[[Online Etymology Dictionary]]}}</ref> The word ''crap'' ultimately comes from [[Medieval Latin]] ''crappa''.<ref name="Harper 2010" /><ref>{{cite encyclopedia |title=Crap |encyclopedia=[[American Heritage Dictionary of the English Language]] |publisher=[[Houghton Mifflin Harcourt]] |url=http://education.yahoo.com/reference/dictionary/entry/crap |access-date=June 17, 2011 |date=2001 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130121195235/http://education.yahoo.com/reference/dictionary/entry/crap |archive-date=January 21, 2013}}</ref>
* The word ''[[fuck]]'' did not originate in the Middle Ages as an [[acronym]].<ref name="Sheidlower 2009">{{#invoke:Cite|book|last=Sheidlower |first=Jesse |author-link=Jesse Sheidlower |title=[[The F-Word (book)|The F-Word]] |publisher=[[Oxford University Press]] |year=2009 |isbn=978-0-19-539311-8 |edition=3rd |location=New York |chapter=Introduction}}</ref> Proposed acronyms include "fornicating under consent of king" or "for unlawful carnal knowledge", purportedly used as a sign posted above adulterers in the [[stocks]]. Nor did it originate as a corruption of "pluck yew" (an idiom falsely attributed to the English for drawing a [[longbow]]).<ref name="Sheidlower 2009" /><ref name="fuckingcitation">a. {{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Mikkelson |first=Barbara |date=July 8, 2007 |title=What the Fuck? |url=http://www.snopes.com/language/acronyms/fuck.asp |url-status=live |archive-url=https://www.webcitation.org/67BKzr94Z?url=http://www.snopes.com/language/acronyms/fuck.asp |archive-date=April 25, 2012 |access-date=June 17, 2011 |website=[[Snopes]]}}
<br />b. {{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Mikkelson |first=Barbara |date=July 9, 2007 |title=Pluck Yew |url=http://www.snopes.com/language/apocryph/pluckyew.asp |url-status=live |archive-url=https://www.webcitation.org/67BKzr94Z?url=http://www.snopes.com/language/acronyms/fuck.asp |archive-date=April 25, 2012 |access-date=June 17, 2011 |website=[[Snopes]]}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|journal|last=Sheidlower |first=Jesse |author-link=Jesse Sheidlower |date=Autumn 1998 |title=Revising the F-Word |journal=Verbatim: The Language Quarterly |volume=23 |issue=4 |pages=18–21}}</ref> It is most likely derived from [[Middle Dutch]] or other [[Germanic languages]], where it either meant "to thrust" or "to copulate with" (''fokken'' in Middle Dutch), "to copulate", or "to strike, push, copulate" or "penis".<ref name="fuckingcitation" /> Either way, these variations would have been derived from the Indo-European root word ''-peuk'', meaning "to prick".<ref name="fuckingcitation" />
* The expression "[[rule of thumb]]" did not originate from an English law allowing a man to beat his wife with a stick no thicker than his thumb, and there is no evidence that such a law ever existed.<ref name="Kelly 1994">{{#invoke:Cite|journal|last=Kelly |first=Henry Ansgar |date=September 1994 |title=''Rule of Thumb'' and the Folklaw of the Husband's Stick |url=http://csswashtenaw.org/wp-content/uploads/2013/07/Rule_of_Thumb.pdf |url-status=live |journal=Journal of Legal Education |volume=44 |issue=3 |pages=341–65 |jstor=42893341 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150924122340/http://csswashtenaw.org/wp-content/uploads/2013/07/Rule_of_Thumb.pdf |archive-date=September 24, 2015 |access-date=May 23, 2024}}</ref> The expression originates from the late seventeenth century from various [[Craft|trades]] where quantities were measured by comparison to the width or length of a thumb.<ref name="Kelly 1994" />{{sfn|O'Conner|Kellerman|2009|pp=123–126}}
* The term "[[blue law]]s", denoting laws banning certain activities on specific days, did not originate from such laws being originally written on blue paper.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Mikkelson |first=David |date=March 11, 2001 |title=Why Are Blue Laws Called 'Blue Laws'? |url=https://www.snopes.com/fact-check/what-are-blue-laws/ |access-date=June 25, 2024 |website=[[Snopes]]}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|last=Cheung |first=Iva |date=May 25, 2015 |title=Sacré Bleu! Why Is Blue the Most Profane Color? |url=https://slate.com/human-interest/2015/05/why-do-we-associate-the-color-blue-with-swearing.html |access-date=June 25, 2024 |work=[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]}}</ref>
* The word ''the'' was never pronounced or spelled "ye" in [[Old English|Old]] or [[Middle English]].<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Brians |first=Paul |date=2011 |title=Common Errors in English Usage – Ye |url=http://public.wsu.edu/~brians/errors/ye.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130531122316/http://public.wsu.edu/~brians/errors/ye.html |archive-date=May 31, 2013 |access-date=July 7, 2024 |work=Common Errors in English Usage |publisher=[[Washington State University]]}}</ref> The confusion, seen in the [[stock phrase|common stock phrase]] "[[ye olde]]", derives from the use of the character [[Thorn (letter)|thorn]] (þ), which in Middle English represented the sound now represented in Modern English by "th".<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Harper |first=Douglas |date=2001–2010 |title=Etymology Online |url=http://www.etymonline.com/index.php?term=ye |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140427082431/http://www.etymonline.com/index.php?term=ye |archive-date=April 27, 2014 |access-date=June 24, 2011 |work=Online Etymology Dictionary}}</ref> This evolved as early printing presses replaced the word ''the'' with "yͤ", a "y" character with a superscript "e".<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|book|last=Hill |first=Will |title=The Routledge Handbook of the English Writing System |date=30 June 2020 |publisher=[[Taylor & Francis]] |isbn=978-0-367-58156-5 |pages=6, 15 |chapter=Chapter 25: Typography and the printed English text |chapter-url=https://arro.anglia.ac.uk/id/eprint/703215/1/25HillFinalDV.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220710022857/https://arro.anglia.ac.uk/id/eprint/703215/1/25HillFinalDV.pdf |archive-date=July 10, 2022 }}</ref>
* The [[anti-Italian]] slur ''[[wop]]'' did not originate from an acronym for "without papers"<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|last=Zimmer |first=Ben |author-link=Ben Zimmer |date=April 23, 2018 |title='Wop' Doesn't Mean What Andrew Cuomo Thinks It Means |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/politics/archive/2018/04/wop-doesnt-mean-what-andrew-cuomo-thinks-it-means/558659/ |access-date=June 22, 2024 |work=[[The Atlantic]]}}</ref> or "without passport";<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Harper |first=Douglas |date=October 25, 2017 |title=Ingenious Trifling |url=http://www.etymonline.com/baloney.php |access-date=June 16, 2024 |website=[[Online Etymology Dictionary]]}}</ref>{{sfn|O'Conner|Kellerman|2009|pages=77, 145}} it is actually derived from the term ''[[guappo]]'' (roughly meaning ''[[Thuggee|thug]]''), from the Spanish ''guapo''.<ref>{{Cite American Heritage Dictionary|wop}}</ref>

== Law, crime, and military ==
[[File:ViolentCrimeInTheUS1960-2022.png|thumb|Violent crime rates in the United States declined between 1991 and 2022.]]
* [[Crime drop|Crime rates are declining]] for most types of crime, beginning in the mid to late 1980s and early 1990s.<ref name=cj>{{cite journal|last1=Farrell|first1=Graham|last2=Tilley|first2=Nick|last3=Tseloni|first3=Andromachi|title=Why the Crime Drop?|journal=Crime and Justice|date=September 2014|volume=43|issue=1|pages=421–490|doi=10.1086/678081|s2cid=145719976|url=http://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/97466/1/Farrell%20Tilley%20Tseloni%202014%20Why%20the%20Crime%20Drop%20%28C%26J%20vol43%29.pdf|archive-date=December 15, 2021|access-date=October 7, 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211215225328/https://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/97466/1/Farrell%20Tilley%20Tseloni%202014%20Why%20the%20Crime%20Drop%20%28C%26J%20vol43%29.pdf|url-status=live}}</ref> In Europe, [[crime statistics]] show this is part of a broader pattern of crime decline since the late [[Middle Ages]], with a reversal from the 1960s to the 1980s and 1990s, before the decline continued.<ref name=tonry-1-2>{{Cite journal |url=https://scholarship.law.umn.edu/faculty_articles/511 |last=Tonry |first=Michael |title=Why Crime Rates Are Falling Throughout the Western World, 43 Crime & Just. 1 (2014) |journal=Crime & Just |date=January 2014 |pages=1–2 |archive-date=May 23, 2020 |access-date=October 7, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200523194607/https://scholarship.law.umn.edu/faculty_articles/511/ |url-status=live }}</ref> [[Crime in the United States|In the United States]], between 1993 and 2022, the rate of violent crime per 100,000 people fell by almost 50%, and the rate of property crime fell by more than half.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Gramlich |first=John |date=November 20, 2020 |title=What the data says (and doesn't say) about crime in the United States |url=https://www.pewresearch.org/fact-tank/2020/11/20/facts-about-crime-in-the-u-s/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210218051336/https://www.pewresearch.org/fact-tank/2020/11/20/facts-about-crime-in-the-u-s/ |archive-date=February 18, 2021 |access-date=February 19, 2021 |website=[[Pew Research]]}}</ref> The number of gun homicides also decreased.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|date=October 21, 2015 |title=Gun homicides steady after decline in '90s; suicide rate edges up |url=https://www.pewresearch.org/fact-tank/2015/10/21/gun-homicides-steady-after-decline-in-90s-suicide-rate-edges-up/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190903161457/https://www.pewresearch.org/fact-tank/2015/10/21/gun-homicides-steady-after-decline-in-90s-suicide-rate-edges-up/ |archive-date=September 3, 2019 |access-date=September 4, 2019 |website=[[Pew Research]]}}</ref>
* Studies consistently find that [[corporal punishment]] may have the opposite of its intended effect in the long run, decreasing long-term [[obedience (human behavior)|obedience]],<ref name=Gershoff2010>{{cite journal |author=Gershoff, Elizabeth T. |date=Spring 2010 |title=More Harm Than Good: A Summary of Scientific Research on the Intended and Unintended Effects of Corporal Punishment on Children |url=http://scholarship.law.duke.edu/lcp/vol73/iss2/3/ |journal=[[Law & Contemporary Problems]] |publisher=[[Duke University School of Law]] |volume=73 |issue=2 |pages=31–56 |author-link=Elizabeth Gershoff |archive-date=June 20, 2017 |access-date=December 18, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170620170605/http://scholarship.law.duke.edu/lcp/vol73/iss2/3/ |url-status=live }}</ref> while increasing the chances of [[aggressive]] behavior, [[Depression (mood)|depression]], [[anxiety]], [[suicide]], and [[physical abuse]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gershoff |first1=Elizabeth T. |last2=Grogan-Kaylor |first2=Andrew |title=Spanking and Child Outcomes: Old Controversies and New Meta-Analyses |journal=Journal of Family Psychology |date=2016 |doi=10.1037/fam0000191 |pmid=27055181 |volume=30 |issue=4 |pages=453–469 |pmc=7992110 |url=http://local.psy.miami.edu/faculty/dmessinger/c_c/rsrcs/rdgs/emot/spanging.meta.2016.jFamPsych.pdf |archive-date=April 18, 2024 |access-date=December 18, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240418063144/https://local.psy.miami.edu/faculty/dmessinger/c_c/rsrcs/rdgs/emot/spanging.meta.2016.jFamPsych.pdf |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="Afifi">{{cite journal | last1=Afifi | first1=T. O. | last2=Mota | first2=N. P. | last3=Dasiewicz | first3=P. | last4=MacMillan | first4=H. L. | last5=Sareen | first5=J. | title=Physical Punishment and Mental Disorders: Results From a Nationally Representative US Sample | journal=Pediatrics | publisher=American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) | volume=130 | issue=2 | date=2 July 2012 | issn=0031-4005 | doi=10.1542/peds.2011-2947 | pmid=22753561 | pages=184–192| s2cid=21759236 | doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Gershoff |first=E. T. |title=The strength of the causal evidence against physical punishment of children and its implications for parents, psychologists, and policymakers. |url=https://psycnet.apa.org/manuscript/2018-33351-006.pdf |journal=American Psychologist |date=2018 |volume=73 |issue=5 |pages=626–638 |doi=10.1037/amp0000327 |pmid=29999352 |pmc=8194004 |archive-date=September 23, 2024 |access-date=December 18, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240923102122/https://psycnet.apa.org/manuscript/2018-33351-006.pdf |url-status=live }}</ref> See also [[Spanking#Alternatives to spanking|Alternatives to spanking]].
* Chewing gum is not punishable by [[caning]] in Singapore. Although importing and selling chewing gum [[Chewing gum sales ban in Singapore|has been illegal in Singapore]] since 1992, and [[Caning in Singapore|corporal punishment]] is still an applicable penalty for certain offenses in the country, the two facts are unrelated; chewing gum-related offenses have always been only subject to fines and incarceration, and the possession or consumption of chewing gum itself is not illegal.<ref>a. {{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Benedictus |first=Leo |date=March 23, 2015 |title=Gum control: how Lee Kuan Yew kept chewing gum off Singapore's streets |url=http://www.theguardian.com/lifeandstyle/shortcuts/2015/mar/23/gum-control-how-lee-kuan-yew-kept-chewing-gum-off-singapores-streets |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230210125248/https://www.theguardian.com/lifeandstyle/shortcuts/2015/mar/23/gum-control-how-lee-kuan-yew-kept-chewing-gum-off-singapores-streets |archive-date=February 10, 2023 |access-date=July 9, 2022 |website=[[The Guardian]] |language=en}}<br />b. {{#invoke:Cite|journal|last=Rajah |first=Jothie |date=January 1, 2014 |title=Flogging Gum: Cultural Imaginaries and Postcoloniality in Singapore's Rule of Law |url=https://ro.uow.edu.au/ltc/vol18/iss1/10 |url-status=live |journal=Law Text Culture |volume=18 |issue=1 |pages=135–165 |doi=10.14453/ltc.558 |issn=1322-9060 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310232148/https://ro.uow.edu.au/ltc/vol18/iss1/10/ |archive-date=March 10, 2023 |access-date=July 9, 2022}}<br />c. {{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Brown |first=Lauren |date=March 1, 2012 |title=How To Travel In Singapore Without Getting Caned |url=https://www.businessinsider.com/singapore-rules-laws-etiquette-gum-drugs-2012-2 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221021083211/https://www.businessinsider.com/singapore-rules-laws-etiquette-gum-drugs-2012-2 |archive-date=October 21, 2022 |access-date=July 9, 2022 |website=[[Business Insider]] |language=en-US}}</ref>
* Employees of the international police organization [[Interpol]] cannot conduct investigations, use [[fake passport]]s or arrest criminals. However, Interpol does issue [[Interpol Travel Document|travel documents]] to its officials in some circumstances. Interpol's role is facilitating international communication between [[Law enforcement agency|law enforcement agencies]] of [[sovereign state]]s.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Gilsinan |first=Kathy |date=2014-05-12 |title=Interpol at 100: Does the World's Police Force Work? |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/international/archive/2014/05/interpol-the-global-police-force-that-isnt/362086/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230416134627/https://www.theatlantic.com/international/archive/2014/05/interpol-the-global-police-force-that-isnt/362086/ |archive-date=April 16, 2023 |access-date=2022-04-02 |website=[[The Atlantic]] |language=}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Lee |first=Michael |title=Interpol hopes physical border security will solve virtual borders |url=https://www.zdnet.com/article/interpol-hopes-physical-border-security-will-solve-virtual-borders/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230129045835/https://www.zdnet.com/article/interpol-hopes-physical-border-security-will-solve-virtual-borders/ |archive-date=January 29, 2023 |access-date=2022-04-07 |website=[[ZDNet]] |language=en}}</ref>
* No cases have been proven of strangers [[Poisoned candy myths|killing or permanently injuring children by intentionally hiding]] poisons or sharp objects such as razor blades in candy or apples during Halloween trick-or-treating. However, in at least one case, adult family members have spread this story to cover up [[filicide]].<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|book|last=Brunvand |first=Jan Harold |author-link=Jan Harold Brunvand |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9xOb-19lXx8C |title=Encyclopedia of Urban Legends |date=2012-01-01 |publisher=[[ABC-CLIO]] |isbn=978-1-59884-720-8 |page=288 |language=en |access-date=November 10, 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230121033601/https://books.google.com/books?id=9xOb-19lXx8C |archive-date=January 21, 2023 |url-status=live}}</ref>
* There has never been a documented case of [[Black cat#Adoption and Black Cat Day|pet black cats being tortured or ritually sacrificed]] around Halloween. Where violent deaths of black cats have been documented around Halloween, the death has usually been ascribed to natural predators, such as coyotes, eagles, or raptors.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.dcourier.com/main.asp?SectionID=74&SubsectionID=114&ArticleID=86024 |author=Boks, Ed |title=The truth about black cats and Halloween |location=Prescott, Arizona |work=[[The Daily Courier (Arizona)|The Daily Courier]] |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120322011039/http://www.dcourier.com/main.asp?SectionID=74&SubsectionID=114&ArticleID=86024 |archive-date=2012-03-22 |date=2010-10-06 |access-date=2010-10-10 }}</ref>
* It is not necessary to wait 24 hours before filing a [[missing person]] report. When there is evidence of violence or of an unusual absence, it is important to start an investigation promptly.<ref>a. {{#invoke:Cite|news|last1=Sparks |first1=Preston |last2=Cox |first2=Timothy |date=November 17, 2008 |title=Missing persons usually found |url=http://chronicle.augusta.com/stories/2008/11/17/met_483813.shtml |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131214111833/http://chronicle.augusta.com/stories/2008/11/17/met_483813.shtml |archive-date=December 14, 2013 |access-date=May 21, 2011 |newspaper=[[Augusta Chronicle]]}}
<br />b. {{#invoke:Cite|web|title=FAQs: Question: Do you need to wait 24&nbsp;hours before reporting a person missing? |url=http://www.missingpersons.gov.au/nmpcc/faqs.aspx#a1 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080923232230/http://www.missingpersons.gov.au/nmpcc/faqs.aspx#a1 |archive-date=September 23, 2008 |access-date=May 22, 2011 |publisher=National Missing Persons Coordination Center, [[Australian Federal Police]]}}<br />c. {{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Vongkiatkajorn |first=Kanyakrit |title=NYPD: How The Police Handles Missing Persons Cases |url=https://themissingny.nycitynewsservice.com/part-two/nypd-explainer/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230404005845/https://themissingny.nycitynewsservice.com/part-two/nypd-explainer/ |archive-date=April 4, 2023 |access-date=June 25, 2022 |website=[[NYCity News Service]]}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|date=June 3, 2013 |title=Report or find a missing person |url=https://www.gov.uk/report-missing-person |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140122170802/https://www.gov.uk/report-missing-person |archive-date=January 22, 2014 |access-date=August 18, 2013 |website=[[Gov.uk]]}}</ref> [[Criminology]] experts say the first 72 hours in a missing person investigation are the most critical.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|title=Why the first 72 hours in a missing persons investigation are the most critical, according to criminology experts |url=https://abcnews.go.com/US/72-hours-missing-persons-investigation-critical-criminology-experts/story?id=58292638 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230312194816/https://abcnews.go.com/US/72-hours-missing-persons-investigation-critical-criminology-experts/story?id=58292638 |archive-date=March 12, 2023 |access-date=June 11, 2022 |website=[[ABC News (United States)|ABC News]]}}</ref>
* [[Perry Mason moment]]s, in which a person on trial for a crime is suddenly exonerated by newly introduced revelations, are exceptionally rare in real-life court proceedings, despite their ubiquity in [[legal drama]]. The vast majority of evidence is unveiled in pretrial [[Discovery (law)|discovery]]; should new revelations occur, a trial is usually [[stay of proceedings|stayed]] until both the prosecution and defense can review it.<ref>{{Bluebook journal|first=Douglas|last=Abrams|title=References to Television Shows in Judicial Opinions and Written Advocacy (Part I)|volume=75|journal=J.Mo.B.|page=25|pin=27|year=2019|url=https://scholarship.law.missouri.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1959&context=facpubs|quote=... [W]ell prepared counsel normally anticipate the course of the trial in the relatively few cases that do not end with pretrial settlement or plea bargain, and because contemporary procedure emphasizes pretrial discovery and favors conferences with the court outside the jury's presence or earshot when surprise does surface.}}</ref>
* [[Diplomatic mission#Extraterritoriality|Diplomatic missions]] do not necessarily enjoy full [[extraterritorial status]] and are generally not sovereign territory of the represented state. The sending state can give embassies sovereign status but this only happens with a minority of countries. Although they receive special privileges (such as immunity from most local laws) by the [[Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations]], the premises of an embassy remain under the jurisdiction of the host state.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://integrity-legal.com/legal-blog/miscellaneous/laws-and-rules-regarding-extraterritoriality/ |title=Laws and Rules Regarding Extraterritoriality |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210414125434/http://integrity-legal.com/legal-blog/miscellaneous/laws-and-rules-regarding-extraterritoriality/ |archive-date=2021-04-14 |url-status=dead |website=integrity-legal.com |quote=There is a common misconception that Embassies and Consulates have extraterritoriality. As anecdotal evidence of this misconception, people will often say things like, 'the US Embassy sits upon United States soil.' For the most part, this is not the case as extraterritoriality is not conferred upon an Embassy or Consulate, but in some situations extraterritoriality may be created by Treaty.}}</ref>
* The age of majority - the legal threshold of adulthood - is rarely a single, uniform age for every legal right and responsibility. While most countries define 18 as the age where minors cease to be under parental control and gain full civil capacity (like voting or contracting), specific activities are staggered at different ages. In many jurisdictions the legal age to buy alcohol is 21, even though legal adulthood is 18. Many countries require individuals to be 25 or older to run for high-ranking government positions. Gambling in casinos often requires the individual to be over 21 in many countries. In some countries, adopting a child requires the person to be at least 21. Learner licenses for driving vehicles are often allowed at 15-16, with full, unrestricted driving licenses typically available at 17-18.

=== United States ===
[[File:Arrest rates from 2012 to 2018 in Texas.png|thumb|Immigrants had lower arrest rates than citizens in Texas, 2012–2018]]

* Undocumented immigrants in the US have substantially lower [[Crime in the United States#Arrests by immigration status|crime rates]] than US-born citizens.<ref>Immigrants are less likely to commit crimes than U.S.-born Americans, studies find MARCH 8,  ALL THINGS CONSIDERED Jasmine Garsd https://www.npr.org/2024/03/08/1237103158/immigrants-are-less-likely-to-commit-crimes-than-us-born-americans-studies-find {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240516223924/https://www.npr.org/2024/03/08/1237103158/immigrants-are-less-likely-to-commit-crimes-than-us-born-americans-studies-find |date=May 16, 2024 }}</ref><ref name="Light 2020" /> Compared to undocumented immigrants, US-born citizens are more than twice as likely to be arrested for violent crimes, 2.5 times more likely to be arrested for [[Drug-related crime|drug crimes]], and over 4 times more likely to be arrested for property crimes.<ref name="Light 2020">{{cite journal |last1=Light |first1=Michael T. |last2=He |first2=Jingying |last3=Robey |first3=Jason P. |title=Comparing crime rates between undocumented immigrants, legal immigrants, and native-born US citizens in Texas |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |date=2020 |volume=117 |issue=51 |pages=32340–32347 |doi=10.1073/pnas.2014704117 |doi-access=free|pmid=33288713 |pmc=7768760 |bibcode=2020PNAS..11732340L }}</ref> Immigrants are 60% less likely to be incarcerated than US-born citizens.<ref>The mythical tie between immigration and crime
 Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research (SIEPR) Krysten Crawford  July 21, 2023 https://siepr.stanford.edu/news/mythical-tie-between-immigration-and-crime {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240521185535/https://siepr.stanford.edu/news/mythical-tie-between-immigration-and-crime |date=May 21, 2024 }}</ref>
* The [[First Amendment to the United States Constitution]] generally prevents only government restrictions on the freedoms of [[Freedom of religion in the United States|religion]], [[Freedom of speech in the United States|speech]], [[Freedom of the press in the United States|press]], [[Freedom of assembly|assembly]], or [[Right to petition in the United States|petition]],<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|last1=Willingham |first1=AJ |date=September 6, 2018 |title=The First Amendment doesn't guarantee you the rights you think it does |url=https://www.cnn.com/2017/04/27/politics/first-amendment-explainer-trnd/index.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190301225218/https://www.cnn.com/2017/04/27/politics/first-amendment-explainer-trnd/index.html |archive-date=March 1, 2019 |access-date=March 1, 2019 |work=CNN}}</ref> not restrictions imposed by other entities unless they are [[state actor|acting on behalf of the government]].<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|last1=McGregor |first1=Jena |date=August 8, 2017 |title=The Google memo is a reminder that we generally don't have free speech at work |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/on-leadership/wp/2017/08/08/the-google-memo-is-a-reminder-that-we-generally-dont-have-free-speech-at-work/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200125155407/https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/on-leadership/wp/2017/08/08/the-google-memo-is-a-reminder-that-we-generally-dont-have-free-speech-at-work/ |archive-date=January 25, 2020 |access-date=March 1, 2019 |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last1=Dunn |first1=Christopher |date=April 28, 2009 |title=Column: Applying the Constitution to Private Actors (New York Law Journal) |url=http://www.nyclu.org/oped/column-applying-constitution-private-actors-new-york-law-journal |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131202234721/http://www.nyclu.org/oped/column-applying-constitution-private-actors-new-york-law-journal |archive-date=December 2, 2013 |access-date=January 27, 2017 |publisher=New York Civil Liberties Union}}</ref> Other laws may limit the ability of private businesses and individuals to restrict the speech of others.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|last1=Berman-Gorvine |first1=Martin |date=May 19, 2014 |title=Employer Ability to Silence Employee Speech Narrowing in Private Sector, Attorneys Say |url=https://www.bna.com/employer-ability-silence-n17179890580/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190331165028/https://www.bna.com/employer-ability-silence-n17179890580/ |archive-date=March 31, 2019 |access-date=March 1, 2019 |work=Bloomberg BNA}}</ref>
* In the United States, a defendant may not have their case dismissed simply because they were not read their [[Miranda warning|Miranda rights]] at the time of their arrest.<ref>Imwinkelried and Blinka, ''Criminal Evidentiary Foundations'', 2d ed. (Lexis 2007) {{ISBN|978-1-4224-1741-6}} at 620.</ref> If a person is not given a Miranda warning before the interrogation is conducted, statements made by them during the interrogation may not be admissible in a trial, but the prosecution may still present other forms of evidence, or statements made during interrogations where the defendant ''was'' read their Miranda rights.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|date=September 10, 2021 |title=Can a case be dismissed if a person is not read his/her Miranda rights? |url=http://www.mirandawarning.org/casedismissedifnotreadmirandarights.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220714054454/http://www.mirandawarning.org/casedismissedifnotreadmirandarights.html |archive-date=July 14, 2022 |access-date=September 10, 2021 |work=Patrick Barone}}</ref>
* Police officers in the United States are not required to identify themselves as police in undercover work, and may lie when engaged in such work.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|date=March 12, 1998 |title=Snopes on Entrapment |url=http://www.snopes.com/risque/hookers/cop.asp |access-date=August 29, 2009 |website=Snopes.com}}</ref> Claiming [[entrapment]] as a defense depends on whether the defendant was improperly induced by undue pressure from government agents (or others acting under police direction) to commit crimes they would not have otherwise committed.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last1=Larson |first1=Aaron |title=What is Entrapment |url=https://www.expertlaw.com/library/criminal-law/what-entrapment |website=ExpertLaw |access-date=June 18, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171026002224/https://www.expertlaw.com/library/criminal-law/what-entrapment |archive-date=October 26, 2017 }}</ref>
* It is not illegal in the US to [[Shouting fire in a crowded theater|shout "fire" in a crowded theater]]. Although this is often given as an example of speech that is not protected by the First Amendment, it is not now nor has it ever been binding law. The phrase originates from Justice [[Oliver Wendell Holmes Jr.]]'s [[legal opinion|opinion]] in the [[United States Supreme Court]] case ''[[Schenck v. United States]]'' in 1919, which held that the defendant's speech in opposition to the draft during World War I was not protected free speech. However, that case was not about shouting "fire" and the decision was later overturned by ''[[Brandenburg v. Ohio]]'' in 1969.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Timm |first=Trevor |date=2012-11-02 |title=It's Time to Stop Using the 'Fire in a Crowded Theater' Quote |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/national/archive/2012/11/its-time-to-stop-using-the-fire-in-a-crowded-theater-quote/264449/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230815121915/https://www.theatlantic.com/national/archive/2012/11/its-time-to-stop-using-the-fire-in-a-crowded-theater-quote/264449/ |archive-date=August 15, 2023 |access-date=2020-06-20 |website=The Atlantic |language=en-US}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|last=Volokh |first=Eugene |date=2015-05-11 |title=Shouting fire in a crowded theater |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/volokh-conspiracy/wp/2015/05/11/shouting-fire-in-a-crowded-theater/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230320072905/https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/volokh-conspiracy/wp/2015/05/11/shouting-fire-in-a-crowded-theater/ |archive-date=March 20, 2023 |access-date=2020-06-20 |newspaper=Washington Post |language=en}}</ref>
* The US Armed Forces have generally forbidden military enlistment as a form of [[deferred adjudication]] (that is, an option for convicts to avoid jail time) since the 1980s.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|date=May 8, 2012 |title=5 Military Myths Busted |url=https://www.military.com/join-armed-forces/military-myths.html |access-date=June 23, 2024 |website=[[Military.com]]}}</ref> US Navy protocols discourage the practice, while the other four branches have specific regulations against it.<ref>a. {{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Powers |first=Rod |date=November 24, 2019 |title=Can a Judge Order Someone to Join the Military or Go to Jail? |url=https://www.thebalance.com/join-the-military-or-go-to-jail-3354033 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171230172616/https://www.thebalance.com/join-the-military-or-go-to-jail-3354033 |archive-date=December 30, 2017 |access-date=December 30, 2017 |website=[[The Balance (website)|The Balance]]}}
<br />b. {{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Schogol |first=Jeff |date=February 3, 2006 |title=Judge said Army or jail, but military doesn't want him |url=https://www.stripes.com/news/judge-said-army-or-jail-but-military-doesn-t-want-him-1.44417 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171230172757/https://www.stripes.com/news/judge-said-army-or-jail-but-military-doesn-t-want-him-1.44417 |archive-date=December 30, 2017 |access-date=December 30, 2017 |website=[[Stars and Stripes (newspaper)|Stars and Stripes]]}}
<br />c. {{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Garnett |first=Benjamin |date=January 21, 2022 |title=Guest columnist: Time to join military or go to jail is over |url=https://www.state-journal.com/opinion/guest-columnist-time-to-join-military-or-go-to-jail-is-over/article_4d29ce28-7926-11ec-9399-3389a56455a2.html |access-date=June 23, 2024 |website=[[The State Journal (Kentucky)|The State Journal]] }}</ref>
* [[Last meal]] requests do not have to be fulfilled.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Mikkelson |first=David |date=October 30, 2014 |title=Death Row Inmate Asks for a Child As His Last Meal, Texas DOC Plan to Grant Request? |url=https://www.snopes.com/fact-check/texas-two-misstep/ |access-date=June 25, 2024 |website=[[Snopes]]}}</ref> States have various restrictions on what can be requested, up to not permitting them at all.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|last=Beam |first=Christopher |date=November 10, 2009 |title=I'll Have 24 Tacos and the Filet Mignon |url=https://slate.com/news-and-politics/2009/11/can-a-prisoner-request-anything-for-his-last-meal.html |access-date=June 25, 2024 |work=[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]}}</ref>
* Although popularly known as the "red telephone", the [[Moscow–Washington hotline]] was never a telephone line, nor were red phones used. The first implementation of the hotline used [[Teleprinter|teletype]] equipment, which was replaced by facsimile (fax) machines in 1988. Since 2008, the hotline has been a secure computer link over which the two countries exchange email.<ref name="richardson">Paul E. Richardson, "The hot line (is a Hollywood myth)", in: ''Russian Life'', September/October issue 2009, pp. 50–59.</ref> Moreover, the hotline links the [[Moscow Kremlin|Kremlin]] to the [[The Pentagon|Pentagon]]—based in [[Arlington County, Virginia]]—and not the [[White House]].<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|journal|last=Clavin |first=Tom |title=There Never Was Such a Thing as a Red Phone in the White House |date=June 18, 2013 |journal=[[Smithsonian (magazine)|Smithsonian]] |url=http://www.smithsonianmag.com/history/there-never-was-such-a-thing-as-a-red-phone-in-the-white-house-1129598/ |access-date=September 21, 2014 |archive-date=November 10, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171110003236/https://www.smithsonianmag.com/history/there-never-was-such-a-thing-as-a-red-phone-in-the-white-house-1129598/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|date=December 13, 2019 |title=Jimi Hendrix cleared of blame for UK parakeet release |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-england-50755015 |access-date=June 26, 2024 |work=[[BBC]]}}</ref>
* Likewise, the [[nuclear football]], the briefcase used by presidents to launch nuclear attacks, does not contain a [[Nuclear button|large red button]] to launch an attack. Rather, its primary use is to confirm the president's identity, and to facilitate communication with [[the Pentagon]].<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|last=Kaplan |first=Fred |date=February 11, 2021 |title=How Close Did the Capitol Rioters Get to the Nuclear "Football"? |url=https://slate.com/news-and-politics/2021/02/nuclear-football-vice-president-pence.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240514011723/https://slate.com/news-and-politics/2021/02/nuclear-football-vice-president-pence.html |archive-date=May 14, 2024 |access-date=June 25, 2024 |work=[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|last=Dobbs |first=Michael |date=October 2014 |title=The Real Story of the "Football" That Follows the President Everywhere |url=https://www.smithsonianmag.com/history/real-story-football-follows-president-everywhere-180952779/ |access-date=June 25, 2024 |work=[[Smithsonian (magazine)|Smithsonian]]}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|last=Craw |first=Victoria |date=January 4, 2018 |title=The nuclear button: Real or fake news? |url=https://www.nzherald.co.nz/world/the-nuclear-button-real-or-fake-news/HTBD2HA4576V6TASNVQQLMCLRE/ |access-date=June 25, 2024 |work=[[The New Zealand Herald]]}}</ref>
* [[Twinkies]] were not [[Twinkie defense|claimed to be the cause]] of San Francisco mayor [[George Moscone]]'s and supervisor [[Harvey Milk]]'s murders. In the trial of [[Dan White]], the defense successfully argued White's diminished capacity as a result of depression. While eating Twinkies was cited as evidence of this depression, it was never claimed to be the cause of the murders.<ref name="Twinkie">{{#invoke:Cite|news|first=Carol |last=Pogash |title=Myth of the 'Twinkie defense' |url=http://www.sfgate.com/cgi-bin/article.cgi?f=/c/a/2003/11/23/INGRE343501.DTL |newspaper=[[San Francisco Chronicle]] |page=D-1 |date=November 23, 2003 |access-date=March 20, 2007 |archive-date=June 11, 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120611032604/http://www.sfgate.com/cgi-bin/article.cgi?f=%2Fc%2Fa%2F2003%2F11%2F23%2FINGRE343501.DTL |url-status=live |quote=The "Twinkie defense" is so ingrained in our culture that it appears in law dictionaries, in sociology textbooks, in college exams and in more than 2, 800 references on Google. Only a few of them call it what it is: a myth.}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Mikkelson |first=David |date=October 30, 1999 |title=The Twinkie Defense: Debunking the Myths and Misinformation |url=https://www.snopes.com/fact-check/the-twinkie-defense/ |access-date=June 18, 2024 |quote="Twinkie defense" is now a widespread and commonly-recognized term. It is also a term based on something that never happened.}}</ref>
* Neither the [[Italian-American Mafia|Mafia]] nor other criminal organizations regularly use or have used [[cement shoes]] to drown their victims.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|author=Colleen Long |date=May 5, 2016 |title=Cops seek killer of man who washed ashore in 'cement shoes' |url=https://philadelphia.cbslocal.com/2016/05/05/cops-seek-killer-of-man-who-washed-ashore-in-cement-shoes/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220518100829/https://philadelphia.cbslocal.com/2016/05/05/cops-seek-killer-of-man-who-washed-ashore-in-cement-shoes/ |archive-date=May 18, 2022 |access-date=August 11, 2018 |work=[[CBS 3 Philadelphia]] |agency=[[Associated Press]]}}</ref> There are only two documented cases of this method being used in murders: one in 1964 and one in 2016 (although, in the former, [[Ernest Rupolo|the victim]] had concrete blocks tied to his legs rather than being enclosed in cement).<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|date=May 5, 2016 |title='Cement shoes' found on NYC corpse |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-36215804#:~:text=%22Cement%20shoes%22%20as%20they%20are,was%20a%20known%20gang%20member. |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230403234806/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-36215804#:~:text=%22Cement%20shoes%22%20as%20they%20are,was%20a%20known%20gang%20member. |archive-date=April 3, 2023 |access-date=August 5, 2021 |work=[[BBC News]]}}</ref> The French Army did use cement shoes on Algerians killed in [[death flights]] during the [[Algerian War]].<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|date=November 20, 2012 |title=Après deux ans de polémique, l'État "enterre" le général Bigeard |url=https://www.france24.com/fr/20121120-polemique-cendres-encore-brulantes-general-bigeard-reposeront-frejus-algerie-torture-indochine |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220706010636/http://www.france24.com/fr/20121120-polemique-cendres-encore-brulantes-general-bigeard-reposeront-frejus-algerie-torture-indochine |archive-date=July 6, 2022 |access-date=June 25, 2022 |website=[[France 24]] |language=fr}}</ref>
* [[Embalming]] is not legally required in the United States.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Mikkelson |first=Barbara |date=June 9, 1999 |title=Have People Been Buried Alive? |url=https://www.snopes.com/fact-check/just-dying-to-get-out/ |website=[[Snopes]]}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|journal|last=Slominski |first=Elena |date=August 29, 2023 |title=Life of the death system: shifting regimes, evolving practices, and the rise of eco-funerals |journal=Sustainability: Science, Practice and Policy |volume=19 |issue=1 |page=7 |article-number=2243779 |doi=10.1080/15487733.2023.2243779 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2023SSPP...1943779S }}</ref> The [[Federal Trade Commission]] passed a rule in 1984 forbidding making this claim, to prevent the funeral industry from promoting the misconception for financial gain.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|journal|last1=Chiappelli |first1=Jeremiah |last2=Chiappelli |first2=Ted |date=December 2008 |title=Drinking Grandma: The Problem of Embalming |journal=Journal of Environmental Health |volume=71 |issue=5 |pages=25–26 |jstor=26327817 |pmid=19115720 }}</ref>
* Half of marriages do not end in [[Divorce in the United States|divorce]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Rodriguez |first=Adrianna |date=September 15, 2025 |title=Everything you think you know about divorce is wrong: The 'divorce myth,' explained |url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/life/health-wellness/2025/09/15/divorce-myth-marriage-trends/85989169007/ |access-date=December 16, 2025 |website=USA Today |language=en-US}}</ref> Rates peaked at around 41% in the 1970s but have since declined.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Hurley |first=Dan |date=April 19, 2005 |title=Divorce Rate: It's Not as High as You Think |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2005/04/19/health/divorce-rate-its-not-as-high-as-you-think.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250930124854/https://www.nytimes.com/2005/04/19/health/divorce-rate-its-not-as-high-as-you-think.html |archive-date=September 30, 2025 |access-date=December 17, 2025 |work=The New York Times |language=en |url-status=live }}</ref> Marriages in more recent generations have been lasting longer and are less likely to end in divorce.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Wrona |first=Aleksandra |date=August 1, 2024 |title=Half of Marriages End in Divorce? |url=https://www.snopes.com//news/2024/08/01/marriage-divorce-rate/ |access-date=December 17, 2025 |website=Snopes |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Shamsian |first=Jacob |date=February 9, 2017 |title=The common statistic that 'half of marriages end in divorce' is bogus |url=https://www.businessinsider.com/what-is-the-divorce-rate-2017-2 |access-date=December 17, 2025 |website=Business Insider |language=en-US}}</ref> Those wed in the 2000s were divorcing at the lowest rates since the 1970s, and it was predicted that eventually around two-thirds of marriages would last if present trends continue.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Miller |first=Claire Cain |date=December 2, 2014 |title=The Divorce Surge Is Over, but the Myth Lives On |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/12/02/upshot/the-divorce-surge-is-over-but-the-myth-lives-on.html |access-date=December 16, 2025 |website=The New York Times}}</ref>

== Literature ==
{{Main|q:Misquotations|l1 = Wikiquote:Misquotations}}
* Many quotations are incorrect or attributed to people who never uttered them, and quotations from obscure or unknown authors are often attributed to more famous figures. Commonly misquoted individuals include [[Mark Twain]], [[Oscar Wilde]], [[Albert Einstein]], [[Adolf Hitler]], [[Winston Churchill]], [[Abraham Lincoln]], [[William Shakespeare]], [[Bill Gates]], [[Steve Jobs]], [[Confucius]], [[Sun Tzu]], and the [[Buddha]].<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|book|last1=Knowles |first1=Elizabeth |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jxFQqDLav6wC |title=What They Didn't Say: A Book of Misquotations |date=October 26, 2006 |publisher=OUP Oxford |isbn=978-0-19-150054-1 |language=en |access-date=January 26, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240523005717/https://books.google.com/books?id=jxFQqDLav6wC |archive-date=May 23, 2024 |url-status=live}}</ref>
* [[Mary Shelley]]'s 1818 novel ''[[Frankenstein]]'' is named after the fictional scientist [[Victor Frankenstein]], who created the sapient creature in the novel, not the creature itself, which is never named in the novel and is now usually called [[Frankenstein's monster]].<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|date=September 12, 2018 |title=200th anniversary of Mary Shelley's Frankenstein |url=https://anulib.anu.edu.au/news-events/news/200th-anniversary-mary-shelleys-frankenstein |access-date=June 22, 2024 |work=[[Australian National University]]}}</ref> However, as later adaptations started to refer to the monster itself as Frankenstein, this usage became well-established, and some no longer regard it as erroneous.<ref name=Evans>{{#invoke:Cite|book|last=Evans|first=Bergen|author-link=Bergen Evans|title=Comfortable Words|quote=All dictionaries now recognize "a Frankenstein" as any monstrous creation that threatens to destroy its creator.|url=https://archive.org/details/comfortablewords00evan|url-access=registration|date=1962|location=New York City|publisher=[[Random House]]}}</ref><ref name=Garner>{{#invoke:Cite|book|last=Garner|first=Bryan A.|title=A dictionary of modern American usage|date=1998|publisher=Oxford University Press|location=New York|isbn=978-0-19-507853-4|url=https://archive.org/details/dictionaryofmode00garn |quote=Today this ubiquitous usage must be accepted as standard}}</ref>
* [[Ernest Hemingway]] did not author the [[flash fiction]] story "[[For sale: baby shoes, never worn]]".<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Haglund |first=David |date=January 31, 2013 |title=Did Hemingway Really Write His Famous Six-Word Story? |url=https://slate.com/culture/2013/01/for-sale-baby-shoes-never-worn-hemingway-probably-did-not-write-the-famous-six-word-story.html |access-date=June 23, 2024 |website=[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]}}</ref> The story was not attributed to him until decades after he died.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Churchwell |first=Sarah |date=June 23, 2019 |title=For sale, baby shoes, never worn — the myth of Ernest Hemingway's short story |url=https://www.thetimes.co.uk/article/for-sale-baby-shoes-never-worn-the-myth-of-ernest-hemingways-short-story-pws8jz6qh |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230405045819/https://www.thetimes.co.uk/article/for-sale-baby-shoes-never-worn-the-myth-of-ernest-hemingways-short-story-pws8jz6qh |archive-date=April 5, 2023 |access-date=April 5, 2023 |website=[[The Times]]}}</ref>

== Music ==
{{See also|Mondegreen#In songs}}

=== Classical music ===
* [[Beethoven]] was never entirely deaf. Even by the end of his life, he could detect certain frequencies, albeit in a very diminished way.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Alberge |first=Dalya |date=2020-02-01 |title='Deaf' genius Beethoven was able to hear his final symphony after all |url=https://www.theguardian.com/music/2020/feb/01/beethoven-not-completely-deaf-says-musicologist |access-date=2025-10-23 |work=The Guardian |language=en-GB |issn=0261-3077}}</ref>
* The [[Interval (music)|musical interval]] [[tritone]] was not banned by the Catholic Church and was not associated with devils during the Middle Ages.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Sorensen |first=Jon |date=February 10, 2014 |title=Did the Vatican Outlaw "The Devil In Music?" |url=https://www.catholic.com/magazine/online-edition/did-the-vatican-outlaw-the-devil-in-music |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220708145933/https://www.catholic.com/magazine/online-edition/did-the-vatican-outlaw-the-devil-in-music |archive-date=July 8, 2022 |access-date=July 8, 2022 |website=[[Catholic Answers]]}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Smith |first1=F. J. |title=Some aspects of the tritone and the semitritone in the ''Speculum Musicae'': the non-emergence of the ''diabolus in musica'' |journal=Journal of Musicological Research |date=1979 |volume=3 |issue=1–2 |pages=63–74 [70] |doi=10.1080/01411897908574507}}</ref> Early medieval music used the tritone in Gregorian chant for certain modes.<ref name="Grove">{{cite encyclopedia |year=2001 |title=Tritone |encyclopedia=[[Grove Music Online]] |publisher=[[Oxford University Press]] |location=Oxford |url=https://www.oxfordmusiconline.com/grovemusic/view/10.1093/gmo/9781561592630.001.0001/omo-9781561592630-e-0000028403 |last=Drabkin |first=William |doi=10.1093/gmo/9781561592630.article.28403 |isbn=978-1-56159-263-0 |url-access=subscription |access-date=May 23, 2024 |archive-date=May 23, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240523005715/https://www.oxfordmusiconline.com/grovemusic/display/10.1093/gmo/9781561592630.001.0001/omo-9781561592630-e-0000028403 |url-status=live}} {{Grove Music subscription}}</ref> [[Guido of Arezzo]] ({{circa|991|1033}}) was the first theorist to discourage the interval.<ref name="Grove" />
* [[Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart|Mozart]] did not die from poisoning, and [[Death of Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart|was not poisoned]] by his colleague [[Antonio Salieri]] or anyone else.<ref>{{Cite book| edition = 1st| publisher = [[HarperCollins]]| isbn = 978-0-06-019046-0| last = Solomon| first = Maynard| author-link = Maynard Solomon| title = Mozart: A Life| location = New York| year = 1995| url = https://archive.org/details/mozartlife0002solo| url-access = registration| oclc = 31435799|page=587}}</ref> The rumor originated soon after Salieri's death and was dramatized in [[Alexander Pushkin]]'s play ''[[Mozart and Salieri (play)|Mozart and Salieri]]'' (1832), and later in the 1979 play ''[[Amadeus (play)|Amadeus]]'' by [[Peter Shaffer]] and the subsequent 1984 film ''[[Amadeus (film)|Amadeus]]''.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|title=Was Mozart actually poisoned by Salieri? |url=https://www.classicfm.com/composers/mozart/guides/did-salieri-murder-mozart-mythbuster/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230403230244/https://www.classicfm.com/composers/mozart/guides/did-salieri-murder-mozart-mythbuster/ |archive-date=April 3, 2023 |access-date=April 12, 2021 |website=Classic fm}}</ref>
* The [[minuets in G major and G minor]] by [[Christian Petzold (composer)|Christian Petzold]] is commonly attributed to [[Johann Sebastian Bach]], although the piece was identified in the 1970s as a movement from a harpsichord suite by Petzold. The misconception stems from ''[[Notebook for Anna Magdalena Bach]]'', a book of sheet music by various composers (mostly Bach) in which the minuet is found.<ref>a. {{Cite Grove |last=Wolff |first=Christoph |title=Bach. III. 7. Johann Sebastian Bach. Works}}
<br />b. {{#invoke:Cite|book|last=Williams |first=Peter F. |title=J.S. Bach: A Life in Music |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |year=2007 |page=158}}
<br />c. {{#invoke:Cite|book|last=Schulenberg |first=David |title=The Keyboard Music of J.S. Bach |year=2006 |page=448}}
<br />d. {{#invoke:Cite|journal|last=Schulze |first=Hans-Joachim |author-link=Hans-Joachim Schulze |year=1979 |title=Ein 'Dresdner Menuett' im zweiten Klavierbüchlein der Anna Magdalena Bach. Nebst Hinweisen zur Überlieferung einiger Kammermusikwerke Bachs. |journal=[[Bach-Jahrbuch]] |volume=65 |pages=45-64, 54-58, 64 |doi=10.13141/bjb.v19791376}}</ref> Compositions that are doubtful as works of Bach are cataloged as "[[BWV Anh.]]", short for "[[Bach-Werke-Verzeichnis]] Anhang" ("Bach works catalogue annex"); the minuet is assigned to BWV Anh. 114.
* [[Mozart effect|Listening to Mozart]] or classical music does not enhance intelligence (or [[IQ]]). A study from 1993 reported a short-term improvement in [[Spatial visualization ability|spatial reasoning]].<ref name="Rauscher-Shaw-Ky-1993">{{#invoke:Cite|journal|last1=Rauscher|first1=Frances H.|last2=Shaw|first2=Gordon L.|last3=Ky|first3=Catherine N.|year=1993|title=Music and spatial task performance|url=http://www.uwosh.edu/psychology/faculty-and-staff/frances-rauscher-ph.d/Rauscher_ShawKy_1993.pdf/view|journal=[[Nature (journal)|Nature]]|volume=365|issue=6447|page=611|doi=10.1038/365611a0|pmid=8413624|bibcode=1993Natur.365..611R|s2cid=1385692|access-date=May 23, 2024|archive-date=January 23, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123151744/https://www.uwosh.edu/psychology/faculty-and-staff/frances-rauscher-ph.d/Rauscher_ShawKy_1993.pdf/view}}</ref><ref name="pryse-phillips">{{#invoke:Cite|book|title=Companion to Clinical Neurology |author=William Pryse-Phillips |year=2003 |isbn=0-19-515938-1 |publisher=Oxford University Press}}, p. 611 defines the term as "Slight and transient improvement in spational[sic] reasoning skills detected in normal subjects as a result of exposure to the music of Mozart, specifically his sonata for two pianos (K448)."</ref> However, the weight of subsequent evidence supports either a null effect or short-term effects related to increases in mood and arousal, with mixed results published after the initial report in ''Nature''.<ref name="bridgett">{{#invoke:Cite|journal|title=Effects of listening to Mozart and Bach on the performance of a mathematical test |pmid=10939064|author1=Bridgett, D.J. |author2=Cuevas, J.|journal=Perceptual and Motor Skills |volume=90|issue=3 Pt 2|year=2000 |pages=1171–1175 |doi=10.2466/pms.2000.90.3c.1171|s2cid=35762220}}</ref><ref name="thompson">{{#invoke:Cite|journal|author1=Thompson, W.F. |author2=Schellenberg, E.G. |author3=Husain, G. |title=Arousal, mood, and the Mozart effect |journal=Psychological Science |volume=12|issue=3|pages=248–251 |pmid=11437309|year=2001|doi=10.1111/1467-9280.00345 |s2cid=17641225}}</ref><ref name="JonesWest2006">{{#invoke:Cite|journal|last1=Jones|first1=Martin H.|last2=West |first2=Stephen D.|last3=Estell|first3=David B. |title=The Mozart effect: Arousal, preference, and spatial performance |journal=Psychology of Aesthetics, Creativity, and the Arts |volume=S|issue=1|year=2006|pages=26–32|doi=10.1037/1931-3896.S.1.26}}</ref><ref name="Steele2000">{{#invoke:Cite|journal|last1=Steele |first1=Kenneth M.|title=Arousal and mood factors in the "Mozart effect"|journal=Perceptual and Motor Skills|volume=91|issue=1 |year=2000|pages=188–190 |doi=10.2466/pms.2000.91.1.188|pmid=11011888|s2cid=21977655 |url=http://www1.appstate.edu/~kms//documents/Arousal.pdf |access-date=2015-11-04|archive-date=2017-01-06|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170106155327/http://www1.appstate.edu/~kms/documents/Arousal.pdf}}</ref>
{{Listen
| filename    = en-us-minute-adjective.ogg
| title       = Minute Waltz
| description = Pronunciation of ''minute'' in "Minute Waltz"
| type        = speech
| pos         = right
}}
* The "[[Minute Waltz]]" by [[Frédéric Chopin]] takes, on average, two minutes to play as originally written.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|book|author1=Frédéric Chopin |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0jQ5lDhToBQC&pg=PR6 |title=Piano works |author2=Joseph Banowetz |publisher=Alfred Music Publishing |year=2000 |isbn=978-0-7692-9854-2 |page=6 |access-date=October 2, 2010}}</ref> Its name comes from the adjective ''[[wikt:minute#Adjective|minute]]'', meaning "small", and not [[Minute|the noun referring to a period of sixty seconds]] spelled the same.<ref name="Hinson2004">{{#invoke:Cite|book|author=Maurice Hinson|title=The Pianist's Dictionary|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ov6znsHlhboC&pg=PA114|access-date=October 2, 2010|year=2004|publisher=Indiana University Press|isbn=978-0-253-21682-3|page=114|quote=This piece bears an erroneous nickname since the story long associated with this nickname presumes the pianist is supposed to play the piece in one minute. The word "minute" means small or little waltz.}}</ref>

=== Popular music ===
* "[[Edelweiss (song)|Edelweiss]]" is not the national anthem of Austria, but an original composition created for the 1959 musical ''[[The Sound of Music]]''.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|title=How Do You Solve a Problem Like Maria? |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/pressoffice/pressreleases/stories/2006/07_july/11/maria_facts.shtml |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140427083144/http://www.bbc.co.uk/pressoffice/pressreleases/stories/2006/07_july/11/maria_facts.shtml |archive-date=April 27, 2014}} BBC.</ref> The Austrian national anthem is "[[Land der Berge, Land am Strome]]" ("Land of the Mountains, Land by the River [<nowiki/>[[Danube]]]").<ref name="Vilain2010">{{#invoke:Cite|book|last=Vilain|first=Robert|author-link=Robert Vilain|title=Words and Music|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9gNrhgXpPyYC&pg=PA24|year=2010|publisher=MHRA|isbn=978-1-907322-08-2|pages=24, 28|access-date=May 14, 2017|archive-date=January 21, 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230121033602/https://books.google.com/books?id=9gNrhgXpPyYC&pg=PA24|url-status=live}}</ref>
* [[The Beatles]]' 1965 appearance at [[Shea Stadium]] was not the first time that a rock concert was played at a large, outdoor sports stadium in the United States; such venues were employed by [[Elvis Presley]] in the 1950s and the Beatles themselves in 1964.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|book|last1=Krell |first1=David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p9f9DwAAQBAJ&pg=PT208 |title=The New York Mets in Popular Culture: Critical Essays |publisher=McFarland |year=2020 |isbn=978-1-4766-8010-1 |access-date=May 23, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240523005712/https://books.google.com/books?id=p9f9DwAAQBAJ&pg=PT208#v=onepage&q&f=false |archive-date=May 23, 2024 |url-status=live}}</ref>
* [[The Monkees]] did not outsell the Beatles' and [[the Rolling Stones]]' combined record sales in 1967. [[Michael Nesmith]] originated the claim in a 1977 interview as a prank.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|date=September 18, 2017 |title=In 1977 Mike Nesmith Fooled the World: When The Monkees Sold More Records Than The Beatles and Rolling Stones Combined |url=https://flashbak.com/in-1977-mike-nesmith-fooled-the-world-when-the-monkees-sold-more-records-than-the-beatles-and-rolling-stones-combined-386535/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180620102100/https://flashbak.com/in-1977-mike-nesmith-fooled-the-world-when-the-monkees-sold-more-records-than-the-beatles-and-rolling-stones-combined-386535/ |archive-date=June 20, 2018 |access-date=November 9, 2017 |work=Flashbak |language=en-US}}</ref>
* [[The Rolling Stones]] were not performing "[[Sympathy for the Devil]]" at the 1969 [[Altamont Free Concert]] when [[Killing of Meredith Hunter|Meredith Hunter]] was stabbed to death by a member of the local [[Hells Angels]] chapter that was serving as security. The stabbing occurred later as the band was performing "[[Under My Thumb]]".<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Cruickshank |first=Douglas |date=January 14, 2002 |title=Sympathy for the Devil |url=http://www.salon.com/2002/01/14/sympathy/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303203823/http://www.salon.com/2002/01/14/sympathy/ |archive-date=March 3, 2016 |access-date=June 25, 2006 |website=Salon.com}}</ref><ref>{{cite magazine |last=Burks |first=John |date=February 7, 1970 |title=Rock & Roll's Worst Day |url=https://www.rollingstone.com/news/story/5934386/rock__rolls_worst_day |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090422123137/http://www.rollingstone.com/news/story/5934386/rock__rolls_worst_day |archive-date=April 22, 2009 |access-date=September 13, 2008 |magazine=Rolling Stone}}</ref>
* [[Concept album]]s did not begin with rock music in the 1960s. The format had already been employed by singers such as [[Frank Sinatra]] in the 1940s and 1950s.<ref>a. {{#invoke:Cite|book|last=Elicker |first=Martina |title=Word and Music Studies: Essays on the Song Cycle and on Defining the Field |publisher=Rodopi |year=2001 |isbn=978-90-420-1565-4 |pages=231–234 |chapter=Concept Albums: Song Cycles in Popular Music |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tVPFUS7FIAQC&pg=PA227}}

b. {{#invoke:Cite|book|last=McKnight-Trontz |first=Jennifer |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SRvQ8odl3RYC |title=Exotiquarium: Album Art from the Space Age |publisher=St. Martin's Press |year=1999 |isbn=978-0-312-20133-3}}

c. {{cite AV media notes |title=In the Wee Small Hours |first=Pete |last=Welding |publisher=Capitol Records, 1998 CD release.}}</ref>
* [[Cass Elliot]] (of [[The Mamas & the Papas]]) did not die from choking on a ham sandwich.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news| url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/entertainment-arts-68949240 | title=Mama Cass 'didn't choke to death on a ham sandwich', daughter says | work=BBC News | date=May 6, 2024 }}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news| url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/world-news/2024/05/06/mama-cass-didnt-choke-to-death-on-ham-sandwich/ | title=Mama Cass 'didn't choke to death' on ham sandwich | newspaper=The Telegraph | date=May 6, 2024 | vauthors = Barton A }}</ref> This falsehood was initiated by her manager who wanted to avoid the implication that her death was associated with substance abuse.<ref name="Zoladz">{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Zoladz|first=Lindsay|title=Cass Elliot's Death Spawned a Horrible Myth. She Deserves Better.|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2024/05/09/arts/music/cass-elliot-mamas-and-the-papas-death.html|work=New York Times|date=May 9, 2024}}</ref>
* [[Phil Collins]] did not write his 1981 hit "[[In the Air Tonight]]" about witnessing someone drowning and then confronting the person in the audience who let it happen. According to Collins himself, it was about his emotions when divorcing from his first wife.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|title=Fact Check: In the Air Tonight |url=https://www.snopes.com/fact-check/in-the-air-tonight/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211226154058/https://www.snopes.com/fact-check/in-the-air-tonight/ |archive-date=December 26, 2021 |access-date=August 8, 2019 |website=[[Snopes]] |date=12 September 2000 }}</ref>
* Popular musicians are not more likely to die at the age of 27. The notion of a "[[27 Club]]" arose after the deaths, in a ten-month period, of [[Jimi Hendrix]], [[Janis Joplin]], and [[Jim Morrison]], and later the deaths of [[Kurt Cobain]] and [[Amy Winehouse]]. Statistical studies have shown that there is no scientific basis for this idea.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|first=Jack |last=Butler |date=July 7, 2021 |url=https://www.nationalreview.com/2021/07/the-myth-of-the-27-club/ |title=The Myth of the 27 Club |work=National Review |access-date=July 4, 2024}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|first=Kelsey |last=McKinney |date=May 23, 2015 |url=https://www.vox.com/2015/3/23/8277271/27-club-real-myth |title=Despite the Huge Myth, Musicians Don't Die at 27 — They Die at 56 |work=Vox |access-date=July 4, 2024}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|first=Arun |last=Starkey |date=October 4, 2021 |url=https://faroutmagazine.co.uk/debunking-the-myths-of-the-27-club/ |title=Debunking the Central Myths of the '27 Club' |work=Far Out Magazine |access-date=July 4, 2024}}</ref>
* The song "[[Puff, the Magic Dragon]]" is not about drugs. Both of the song's co-writers consistently and emphatically denied this interpretation throughout their lives, stating that the song is solely about the loss of childhood innocence.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Sparrow |title=Puff: Still Not a Drug Song |url=https://www.chronogram.com/hudsonvalley/puff-still-not-a-drug-song/Content?oid=2169725 |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20220520094136/https://www.chronogram.com/hudsonvalley/puff-still-not-a-drug-song/Content?oid=2169725 |archive-date=2022-05-20 |access-date=2026-02-03 |work=Chronogram Magazine |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|url = https://www.cnn.com/2025/01/07/entertainment/peter-yarrow-dies-at-86/index.html |title = Peter Yarrow of folk-music trio Peter, Paul and Mary dies at 86 |agency = Associated Press |publisher = CNN |date = January 7, 2025 |access-date = January 7, 2025 }}</ref>

== Religion ==
=== Buddhism ===
{{#invoke:multiple image||
| align             = right
| direction         = horizontal
| image1            = Buddha in Sarnath Museum (Dhammajak Mutra).jpg
| caption1          = Statue of the [[Buddha]] (Siddhartha Gautama)
| image2            = Budai Heshang (37007250780).jpg
| caption2          = Statue of [[Budai]], often incorrectly referred to as the "Buddha"
}}
* The chubby, bald monk with lengthened ears who is often depicted laughing, known as the "fat Buddha" or "laughing Buddha" in the West, is a depiction of the Chinese Buddhist folk hero [[Budai]],<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last1=Peterson |first1=Christopher |title=When Did the Buddha Become Fat? |url=https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/the-good-life/201207/when-did-the-buddha-become-fat |website=Psychology Today |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240523005739/https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/the-good-life/201207/when-did-the-buddha-become-fat |archive-date=May 23, 2024 |access-date=November 1, 2018}}</ref> not Siddhartha Gautama, the historical figure usually referenced by the phrase 'the Buddha.' 'Buddha' is a role or title in Buddhism which can properly be applied to both Budai and Siddhartha, but they are separate figures. Siddhartha is most often depicted as a thin man, concentrated in meditation.

=== Christianity ===
* Jesus was most likely [[Date of the birth of Jesus|not born on December 25]], when [[nativity of Jesus|his birth]] is traditionally celebrated as Christmas. It is more likely that his birth was in either the season of spring or perhaps summer. Although the [[Common Era]] ostensibly counts the years since the birth of Jesus,<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|book|last=Bromiley |first=Geoffrey W. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BW_1mt4oebQC&q=jesus+birth+year+before&pg=PA686 |title=The International Standard Bible Encyclopedia |publisher=Wm. B. Eerdmans Publishing |year=1995 |isbn=978-0-8028-3781-3 |access-date=May 18, 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240523010259/https://books.google.com/books?id=BW_1mt4oebQC&q=jesus+birth+year+before&pg=PA686#v=snippet&q=jesus%20birth%20year%20before&f=false |archive-date=May 23, 2024 |url-status=live}}</ref> it is unlikely that he was born in either AD 1 or 1 BC, as such a numbering system would imply. Modern historians estimate a date closer to between 6 BC and 4 BC.<ref name="JDG324">{{#invoke:Cite|book|last=Dunn |first=James DG |year=2003 |title=Jesus Remembered |url=https://archive.org/details/jesusrememberedc00jame |url-access=limited |publisher=Eerdmans Publishing |page=[https://archive.org/details/jesusrememberedc00jame/page/n342 324]}}</ref>
* The Bible does not say that exactly three [[Biblical Magi|magi]] came to visit the baby Jesus, nor that they were kings, or rode on camels, or that their names were [[Caspar (magus)|Caspar]], [[Melchior (magus)|Melchior]], and [[Balthazar (magus)|Balthazar]], nor what color their skin was.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|last=Boyle |first=Alan |date=December 19, 2006 |title=Revisiting the gospel truth |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/science/cosmic-log/revisiting-gospel-truth-flna6c10405991 |access-date=June 22, 2024 |work=[[NBC News]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite magazine |last=Waxman |first=Olivia |date=December 29, 2020 |title=Here's What History Can Tell Us About the Magi |url=https://time.com/5923009/three-kings-wisemen-history-magi-bible/ |access-date=June 22, 2024 |magazine=[[Time (magazine)|Time]] |archive-date=February 6, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240206011530/https://time.com/5923009/three-kings-wisemen-history-magi-bible/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Mikkelson |first=David |date=November 7, 2000 |title=Fact Check: Three Wise Men |url=https://www.snopes.com/fact-check/three-wise-men/ |access-date=June 22, 2024 |website=[[Snopes]]}}</ref>{{Citation needed|date=June 2024|reason=Skin color not in sources}} Three magi are inferred because three gifts are described, but the Bible says only that there was more than one magus.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|book|author=Schiller, G. |title=Iconography of Christian Art (English translation from German) |date=1971 |publisher=Lund Humphries |isbn=978-0-85331-270-3 |volume=I |page=96}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|book|last=Calvin |first=John |author-link=John Calvin |url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/chr/calvin/cc31/cc31027.htm |title=Calvin's Commentaries, Vol. 31: Matthew, Mark and Luke, Part I, tr. by John King |access-date=May 15, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080906104902/http://sacred-texts.com/chr/calvin/cc31/cc31027.htm |archive-date=September 6, 2008 |url-status=live}} Quote from Commentary on Matthew 2:1–6</ref><ref>{{Cite news |title=Magi, Wise Men, or Kings? It’s Complicated. |url=http://www.christianitytoday.com:80/history/holidays/christmas/magi-wise-men-or-kings-its-complicated.html |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20180215224756/http://www.christianitytoday.com:80/history/holidays/christmas/magi-wise-men-or-kings-its-complicated.html |archive-date=2018-02-15 |access-date=2026-02-03 |work=Christian History {{!}} Learn the History of Christianity & the Church |language=en}}</ref>
[[File:Régnier Penitent Mary Magdalene.jpg|thumb|upright|No evidence supports [[Mary Magdalene]] having been a prostitute.<ref name="Ehrman2006">{{#invoke:Cite|book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Tlvgp7WggvEC&q=Ehrman+%22to+you+as+apostles%22&pg=PA253|title=Peter, Paul, and Mary Magdalene: The Followers of Jesus in History and Legend|last=Ehrman|first=Bart D.|date=2006|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-530013-0|location=Oxford, England|pages=189–90|author-link=Bart D. Ehrman|access-date=October 18, 2020|archive-date=January 19, 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230119174748/https://books.google.com/books?id=Tlvgp7WggvEC&q=Ehrman+%22to+you+as+apostles%22&pg=PA253|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name="Spong 2006">{{#invoke:Cite|book|last=Spong |first=John Shelby |url=https://archive.org/details/B-001-024-524-ALL |title=The Sins of Scripture: Exposing the Bible's Texts of Hate to Reveal the God of Love |publisher=HarperOne |date=March 14, 2006 |isbn=978-0-06-077840-8 |page=108 |language=en}}</ref>]]
* The idea that [[Mary Magdalene]] was a prostitute before she met Jesus is not found in the Bible or in any of the other earliest Christian writings. It has been a disputed doctrine in several theological traditions whether Mary Magdalene, [[Mary of Bethany]] (who [[Anointing of Jesus|anoints Jesus' feet]] in John 11:1–12),<ref>{{Cite web |title=oremus Bible Browser : John 11:1–12 |url=https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=John%2011:1%E2%80%9312&version=nrsv |access-date=2026-02-03 |website=bible.oremus.org}}</ref> and the unnamed "[[Jesus and the woman taken in adultery|sinful woman]]" who anoints Jesus' feet in Luke 7:36–50<ref>{{Cite web |title=oremus Bible Browser : Luke 7:36–50 |url=https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Luke%207:36%E2%80%9350&version=nrsv |access-date=2026-02-03 |website=bible.oremus.org}}</ref> were the same woman.<ref name="Ehrman2006" /><ref name="Spong 2006" />
* [[Paul the Apostle]]'s name was not changed from Saul. He was born a Jew, with [[Roman citizenship]] inherited from his father, and thus carried both a Hebrew and a Greco-Roman name from birth, as mentioned by [[Luke the Evangelist|Luke]] in Acts 13:9:<ref>{{Bibleverse|Acts|13:9}}</ref> "...Saul, who also is called Paul...".<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|book|last1=Marrow |first1=Stanley B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RPu1qx50HmgC&pg=PA5 |title=Paul: His Letters and His Theology: an Introduction to Paul's Epistles |publisher=Paulist Press |year=1986 |isbn=978-0-8091-2744-3 |pages=5, 7 |access-date=June 3, 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230801152547/https://books.google.com/books?id=RPu1qx50HmgC&pg=PA5 |archive-date=August 1, 2023 |url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|title=Why did God change Saul's name to Paul? |url=http://www.catholic.com/quickquestions/why-did-god-change-sauls-name-to-paul |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121030000303/http://www.catholic.com/quickquestions/why-did-god-change-sauls-name-to-paul |archive-date=October 30, 2012 |access-date=August 31, 2014 |website=Catholic Answers}}</ref>
* The [[Catholic Church|Roman Catholic]] [[dogma]] of the [[Immaculate Conception]] is unrelated to the Christian doctrine that [[Mary, mother of Jesus|Mary]] [[Fertilisation|conceived]] and [[Virgin birth of Jesus|gave birth to Jesus while remaining a virgin]]. The Immaculate Conception is the belief that {{em|Mary}} was free of [[original sin]] from the moment of her own conception by her parents, [[Joachim]] and [[Saint Anne|Anne]]. A less common mistake is to think that the Immaculate Conception means that Mary herself was conceived without sexual intercourse.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|date=2009 |title=Religion & Ethics – Beliefs: The Immaculate Conception |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/religion/religions/christianity/beliefs/immaculateconception.shtml |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120124033136/http://www.bbc.co.uk/religion/religions/christianity/beliefs/immaculateconception.shtml |archive-date=January 24, 2012 |access-date=January 5, 2011 |publisher=BBC}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|title=Hopko, Thomas. ''The Winter Pascha'' Chapter 9, Antiochian Orthodox Christian Archdiocese of North America |url=http://ww1.antiochian.org/node/18597 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210302142848/http://ww1.antiochian.org/node/18597 |archive-date=March 2, 2021 |access-date=May 23, 2024}}</ref>
* Roman Catholic dogma does not say that the pope is either [[Impeccability#Impeccability and the Pope|sinless]] (as is commonly believed among non-Catholic Christians)<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|title=Papal Infallibility |url=http://www.catholic.com/library/Papal_Infallibility.asp |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090606133529/http://www.catholic.com/library/Papal_Infallibility.asp |archive-date=June 6, 2009 |access-date=June 23, 2024 |website=[[Catholic Answers]]}}</ref> or always infallible.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last1=MacDonald |first1=David |last2=Bonocore |first2=Mark |title=Is the Pope Sinless? |url=http://www.davidmacd.com/catholic/orthodox/pope_bishop_of_rome_primacy.htm#sinless |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110615202522/http://www.davidmacd.com/catholic/orthodox/pope_bishop_of_rome_primacy.htm#sinless |archive-date=June 15, 2011 |access-date=June 17, 2011 |work=The Pope, Bishop of Rome Catholic and Orthodox relations |publisher=CatholicBridge.com}}</ref> Catholic dogma since 1870 does state that a [[divine revelation]] by the pope (generally called {{lang|la|[[ex cathedra]]}}) is free from error, but it does not hold that he is always free from error, even when speaking in his official capacity.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|title=Catholic Encyclopedia: Beatification and Canonization |url=http://www.newadvent.org/cathen/02364b.htm |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180724145034/http://www.newadvent.org/cathen/02364b.htm |archive-date=July 24, 2018 |access-date=August 22, 2016 |website=www.newadvent.org}}</ref>
* Members of [[the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints]] (LDS Church) [[Mormonism and polygamy|no longer practice polygamy]].<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|title=Utah Local News – Salt Lake City News, Sports, Archive |url=http://www.sltrib.com/sltrib/news/54959212-78/believe-bible-church-god.html.csp |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141006070652/http://www.sltrib.com/sltrib/news/54959212-78/believe-bible-church-god.html.csp |archive-date=October 6, 2014 |access-date=October 1, 2014 |newspaper=The Salt Lake Tribune}}
</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|title=Religions – Mormon: Polygamy |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/religion/religions/mormon/socialvalues/polygamy.shtml |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141113021127/http://www.bbc.co.uk/religion/religions/mormon/socialvalues/polygamy.shtml |archive-date=November 13, 2014 |access-date=October 1, 2014 |publisher=BBC}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|title=Mormon church explains polygamy in early days |url=http://bigstory.ap.org/article/mormon-church-explains-polygamy-early-days |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141006140330/http://bigstory.ap.org/article/mormon-church-explains-polygamy-early-days |archive-date=October 6, 2014 |access-date=October 1, 2014 |work=The Big Story}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|title=Mormon Polygamy Misconceptions about Mormon Polygamy |url=http://mormon-polygamy.org/ |archive-url=https://archive.today/20130113111735/http://mormon-polygamy.org/ |archive-date=January 13, 2013 |access-date=October 1, 2014 |work=Mormon Polygamy}}</ref> The Church excommunicates any members who practice polygamy within the organization.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|author=The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints |title=Do Mormons practice polygamy? |url=http://www.mormon.org/faq/practice-of-polygamy |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141006084722/http://www.mormon.org/faq/practice-of-polygamy |archive-date=October 6, 2014 |access-date=October 1, 2014 |work=mormon.org}}</ref> Some [[Mormon fundamentalist]] [[List of sects in the Latter Day Saint movement#Additional churches claiming lineage through Brigham Young and/or founded in the U.S. Intermountain West|sects]] do practice polygamy.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|title=Current practice of polygamy in the Mormon movement |url=http://www.religioustolerance.org/lds_poly2.htm |url-status=live |archive-url=http://arquivo.pt/wayback/20090629085048/http://www.religioustolerance.org/lds_poly2.htm |archive-date=June 29, 2009 |access-date=October 1, 2014}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|date=March 14, 2014 |title=Modern Polygamy: Arizona Mormon Fundamentalists Seek to Shed Stereotypes |url=https://abcnews.go.com/US/modern-polygamy-arizona-mormon-fundamentalists-seek-shed-stereotypes/story?id=19322087 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141006124406/http://abcnews.go.com/US/modern-polygamy-arizona-mormon-fundamentalists-seek-shed-stereotypes/story?id=19322087 |archive-date=October 6, 2014 |access-date=October 1, 2014 |work=ABC News}}</ref>
* The [[First Council of Nicaea]] did not establish the books of the Bible. The Old Testament had likely already been [[Development of the Hebrew Bible canon|established]] by Hebrew scribes before Christ.
