The [[development of the New Testament canon|development]] of the New Testament canon was mostly completed in the third century before the Nicaea Council was convened in 325;<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Roos |first=Dave |date=February 24, 2020 |title=Who Decided Which Books to Include in the Bible? |url=https://people.howstuffworks.com/books-of-bible.htm |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220829150311/https://people.howstuffworks.com/books-of-bible.htm |archive-date=August 29, 2022 |access-date=August 29, 2022 |website=HowStuffWorks |publisher=InfoSpace Holdings}}</ref> it was finalized, along with the [[deuterocanon]], at the [[Council of Rome]] in 382.<ref name="councilofrome">{{#invoke:Cite|book|url=http://www.oxfordreference.com/view/10.1093/acref/9780192802903.001.0001/acref-9780192802903 |title=The Oxford Dictionary of the Christian Church |date=2005-01-01 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-280290-3 |editor-last=Cross |editor-first=F. L. |edition=3rd |page=282 |language=en |chapter=canon of Scripture |doi=10.1093/acref/9780192802903.001.0001 |access-date=September 5, 2022 |editor-last2=Livingstone |editor-first2=E. A. |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121001050958/http://www.oxfordreference.com/view/10.1093/acref/9780192802903.001.0001/acref-9780192802903 |archive-date=October 1, 2012 |url-status=live}}</ref>
* [[Constantine the Great]] did not make Christianity the official religion of the Roman Empire. While he was the first Christian emperor and promoted religious tolerance with the [[Edict of Milan]], Christianity was not declared the official religion of the Roman Empire until AD 380, some 43 years after Constantine's death.<ref name="Constantine Rhodes">{{#invoke:Cite|web| url=https://sites.rhodes.edu/coins/romes-conversion-christianity-and-its-lasting-legacy | title=Rome's Conversion to Christianity and its Lasting Legacy &#124; Rhodes Sites | access-date=May 23, 2024 | archive-date=May 5, 2024 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240505182513/https://sites.rhodes.edu/coins/romes-conversion-christianity-and-its-lasting-legacy | url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="Constantine Cambridge">The Cambridge History of Christianity – Cambridge University Press "Christianity did not become the official religion of the empire under Constantine, as is often mistakenly claimed..." https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/abs/cambridge-history-of-christianity/constantine-and-the-peace-of-the-church/1D6492CD5ECB96174AAE1221F48DC56F {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240505174616/https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/abs/cambridge-history-of-christianity/constantine-and-the-peace-of-the-church/1D6492CD5ECB96174AAE1221F48DC56F |date=May 5, 2024 }}</ref>
* The [[Seven Deadly Sins]] are not listed in the Bible. The concept originated with the early-Christian theologian [[Tertullian]],<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Seven Deadly Sins |url=https://www.catholic.com/magazine/print-edition/the-seven-deadly-sins |access-date=2023-09-30 |website=Catholic Answers |archive-date=August 8, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240808052301/https://www.catholic.com/magazine/print-edition/the-seven-deadly-sins |url-status=live }}</ref>  with [[Pope Gregory I|Gregory I]] listing them in the form that is common today.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Gregory the Great - Moralia in Job (Morals on the Book of Job) - Book XXXI (Book 31) - online |url=http://www.lectionarycentral.com/GregoryMoralia/Book31.html |access-date=2026-02-03 |website=www.lectionarycentral.com}}</ref>

=== Islam ===
{{#invoke:multiple image||
| align             = right
| direction         = vertical
| image1            = Group of Women Wearing Burkas.jpg
| caption1          = Afghan women wearing [[burqa]]s
| image2            = Niqab.jpg
| caption2          = Turkish women wearing [[niqāb]]s
| image3            = Turkish women 2x.jpg
| caption3          = Turkish women wearing [[hijab]]s
}}
* The [[burqa]] (also transliterated as burka or burkha) is often confused with other types of head-wear worn by Muslim women, particularly the [[niqāb]] and the [[hijab]]. A burqa covers the body, head, and face, with a mesh grille to see through. A niqab covers the hair and face, excluding the eyes. A hijab covers the hair and chest but not the face.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|last=Vyver |first=James |date=March 7, 2014 |title=Explainer: Why do Muslim women wear a burka, niqab or hijab? |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2014-09-24/why-do-muslim-women-wear-a-burka-niqab-or-hijab/5761510 |access-date=June 17, 2024 |work=[[Australian Broadcasting Corporation]]}}</ref>
* Not all Muslim women wear face or head coverings.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|date=July 26, 2017 |title=U.S. Muslims Concerned About Their Place in Society, But Continue to Belief in the American Dream |url=https://www.pewresearch.org/religion/2017/07/26/religious-beliefs-and-practices/ |access-date=June 17, 2024 |website=[[Pew Research Center]]}}</ref>
* A [[fatwa]] is a generally non-binding legal opinion issued by an [[Ulama|Islamic scholar]] under [[Sharia|Islamic law]]; it is therefore commonplace for fatwa from different authors to disagree. The misconception that it is a death sentence stems from a decree issued by Ayatollah [[Ruhollah Khomeini]] of Iran in 1989 where he said that the author [[Salman Rushdie]] had earned a death sentence for [[blasphemy]]. It is debated whether this was a fatwa.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|journal|last=Isbister |first=William H. |date=November 23, 2002 |title=A "good" fatwa |journal=[[British Medical Journal]] |volume=325 |issue=7374 |page=1227 |doi=10.1136/bmj.325.7374.1227 |pmc=1124693}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|journal|last=Vultee |first=Fred |date=October 2006 |title=Fatwa on the Bunny |journal=Journal of Communication Inquiry |volume=30 |issue=4 |pages=319–336 |doi=10.1177/0196859906290919 |s2cid=143612009}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|title=In Depth: Islam, Fatwa FAQ |url=http://www.cbc.ca/news/background/islam/fatwa.html |publisher=CBC News Online |date=June 15, 2006|access-date=April 8, 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110714091706/http://www.cbc.ca/news/background/islam/fatwa.html |archive-date=July 14, 2011 }}</ref>
* The word ''[[jihad]]'' does not always mean '[[Religious war|holy war]]'; its literal meaning in Arabic is 'struggle'. While there is such a thing as [[Jihad#Greater and lesser jihad|jihad by the sword]], ''jihad'' can be any spiritual or moral effort or struggle,<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|book|first=Luke|last=Buckles|title=The Complete Idiot's Guide to World Religions|edition=3rd|page=157|publisher=Alpha|date=2004|isbn=978-1-59257-222-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZPokHByS3N0C|access-date=September 21, 2016|archive-date=November 1, 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231101145503/https://books.google.com/books?id=ZPokHByS3N0C|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|url=http://www.kuna.net.kw/NewsAgenciesPublicSite/ArticleDetails.aspx?id=1719934&Language=en|title=Western definition of "jihad" must be corrected – Italian expert|date=March 29, 2007|newspaper=[[Kuwait News Agency]] (KUNA)|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111015041410/http://www.kuna.net.kw/NewsAgenciesPublicSite/ArticleDetails.aspx?id=1719934&Language=en|archive-date=October 15, 2011|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|book|title=Peace and the Limits of War: Transcending the Classical Conception of Jihad |first=Louay M. |last=Safi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1_PFEicd5LkC&pg=PP9 |publisher=[[International Institute of Islamic Thought]] |date=2003 |page=preface |isbn=978-1-56564-402-1 |access-date=September 21, 2016 |archive-date=January 21, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230121033602/https://books.google.com/books?id=1_PFEicd5LkC&pg=PP9 |url-status=live }}</ref> such as seeking knowledge, putting others before oneself, and inviting others to Islam.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|journal|last1=Hoque |first1=M. |last2=Jaim |first2=M. S. |last3=Mohamed |first3=Y. |title=The concept of jihad for education |journal=International Journal of Language and Education |date=30 March 2023 |volume=2 |issue=1 |pages=83–95 |doi=10.33102/alazkiyaa43 |url=https://azkiyaa.usim.edu.my/index.php/jurnal/article/view/43 |access-date=21 January 2024 |doi-access=free |archive-date=May 23, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240523010227/https://azkiyaa.usim.edu.my/index.php/jurnal/article/view/43 |url-status=live }}</ref>
* {{anchor|72 virgins}} The [[Quran]] does not [[Islamophobic trope#72 virgins|promise martyrs 72 virgins in heaven]]. It only mentions that companions, [[houri]], are given to all people in heaven (men and women, martyr or not).<ref>a. {{#invoke:Cite|web|author=al-Jalalayn|title=Tafsir Ar-Rahman|url=https://www.altafsir.com/Tafasir.asp?tMadhNo=1&tTafsirNo=74&tSoraNo=55&tAyahNo=74&tDisplay=yes&UserProfile=0&LanguageId=2|access-date=April 30, 2020|work=[[Tafsir al-Jalalayn]]|archive-date=March 8, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210308002106/https://www.altafsir.com/Tafasir.asp?tMadhNo=1&tTafsirNo=74&tSoraNo=55&tAyahNo=74&tDisplay=yes&UserProfile=0&LanguageId=2|url-status=live}}<br/>b. {{#invoke:Cite|news| url=https://www.theguardian.com/books/2002/jan/12/books.guardianreview5|location=London |work=The Guardian|first=Ibn|last=Warraq|title=Virgins? What virgins?|date=January 12, 2002|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20130622000632/http://www.guardian.co.uk/books/2002/jan/12/books.guardianreview5|archive-date=June 22, 2013| url-status=live}}<br/>c. {{#invoke:Cite|book|title=Urban Terrorism: Myths and Realities | page = 33 | author = Anjali Nirmal | publisher = Pointer Publishers | date = 2009 |isbn=978-81-7132-598-6| url = https://books.google.com/books?id=8EqWnqdsgZMC&pg=PA33}}<br/>d. {{cite news | last1 = Tierney | first1 = Dominic | work = [[The Atlantic]] | date = 23 August 2016 | title = The Twenty Years' War: Two decades ago, Osama bin Laden officially launched al-Qaeda's struggle against the United States. Neither side has won. | url = https://www.theatlantic.com/international/archive/2016/08/twenty-years-war/496736/ | archive-url = https://archive.today/20210403062505/https://www.theatlantic.com/international/archive/2016/08/twenty-years-war/496736/ | archive-date = 2021-04-03 | quote = In his 1996 declaration of war, bin Laden promised that Muslim martyrs would receive 72 pure virgins in heaven. ISIS offers sex slaves right now. Front-loading the rewards proved popular. By 2014, an estimated one thousand foreign fighters were joining ISIS every month, far in excess of new al-Qaeda recruits. }}<br/>e. {{cite news | title = Bin Laden's Fatwa | url = http://www.pbs.org/newshour/updates/military-july-dec96-fatwa_1996/ | work = [[PBS NewsHour]] | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20140419014901/http://www.pbs.org/newshour/updates/military-july-dec96-fatwa_1996/ | archive-date = 2014-04-19 | quote = He also said: "a martyr privileges are guaranteed by Allah; forgiveness with the first gush of his blood, he will be shown his seat in paradise, he will be decorated with the jewels of belief (Imaan), married off to the beautiful ones, protected from the test in the grave, assured security in the day of judgement, crowned with the crown of dignity, a ruby of which is better than this whole world (Duniah) and its entire content, wedded to seventy two of the pure Houries (beautiful ones of Paradise) and his intercession on the behalf of seventy of his relatives will be accepted". Narrated by Ahmad and At-Tirmithi (with the correct and trustworthy reference). }}<br/>f. {{cite web | date = 1996 | title = Milestone Documents in World History: Osama bin Laden's Declaration of Jihad against Americans | url = http://salempress.com/store/pdfs/bin_laden.pdf | publisher = salempress.com | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20170516160932/http://salempress.com/store/pdfs/bin_laden.pdf | archive-date = 2017-05-16 | access-date = 2025-06-25 | url-status = dead }}</ref> Arabic words translated as "virgins" in Western media could be more accurately translated as "angel" or "heavenly being".<ref>{{cite news | author1 = Yotam Feldner | title = 72 Black-Eyed Virgins? | url = https://claremontreviewofbooks.com/72-black-eyed-virgins/ | work = [[Claremont Review of Books]] | quote = CBS translated the Arabic term hur ‘ayn as "virgins". According to two native Arabic speakers, Hafez Al-Mirazi Osman, Washington bureau chief of the Al-Jazeera television network, and Dr. Maher Hathout, a scholar at the Islamic Center of Southern California, the Arabic word has no sexual connotation or gender. Hathout said that a more appropriate translation would be "angel" or "heavenly being". }}</ref>

=== Judaism ===
[[File:Jan Brueghel de Oude en Peter Paul Rubens - Het aards paradijs met de zondeval van Adam en Eva.jpg|thumb|The fruit in the [[Garden of Eden]] is not named in the [[Book of Genesis]].]]
* The [[forbidden fruit]] mentioned in the [[Book of Genesis]] is never identified as an apple,<ref name="Szpek">{{#invoke:Cite|book|author=Szpek, Heidi|title=Voices from the University: The Legacy of the Hebrew Bible|publisher=iUniverse|year=2002|isbn=978-0-595-25619-8|page=92}}</ref> as widely depicted in Western art. The original Hebrew texts mention only ''fruit''.<ref>[[Babylonian Talmud]], [[Berakhot (Talmud)|Berakhot]], 40a</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Adams |first=Cecil |date=November 24, 2006 |title=Was the forbidden fruit in the Garden of Eden an apple? |url=http://www.straightdope.com/columns/read/2682/was-the-forbidden-fruit-in-the-garden-of-eden-an-apple |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140401002830/http://www.straightdope.com/columns/read/2682/was-the-forbidden-fruit-in-the-garden-of-eden-an-apple |archive-date=April 1, 2014 |access-date=February 9, 2012 |website=The Straight Dope}}</ref>
* While tattoos are forbidden by the [[Book of Leviticus]], Jews with tattoos are not [[Religious perspectives on tattooing#Judaism|barred from being buried in a Jewish cemetery]], just as violators of other prohibitions are not barred, as is commonly believed among American Jews.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Levine |first=Rabbi Menachem |date=October 7, 2018 |title=Judaism and Tattoos |work=aishcom |url=http://www.aish.com/jw/s/Judaism-and-Tattoos.html?s=authorart |access-date=December 12, 2018 |publisher=aish.com |archive-date=October 7, 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181007223358/http://www.aish.com/jw/s/Judaism-and-Tattoos.html?s=authorart |url-status=live }}</ref>

== Sports ==
* [[Artificial turf]] is not maintenance free. It requires regular maintenance, such as raking and patching, to keep it functional and safe.<ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Jastifer JR, McNitt AS, Mack CD, Kent RW, McCullough KA, Coughlin MJ, Anderson RB |title=Synthetic Turf: History, Design, Maintenance, and Athlete Safety |journal=Sports Health |volume=11 |issue=1 |pages=84–90 |date=2019 |pmid=30096021 |pmc=6299344 |doi=10.1177/1941738118793378 |type=Review}}</ref>
* The name ''[[golf]]'' is not an acronym for "Gentlemen Only, Ladies Forbidden".<ref name="golfsnopes">{{#invoke:Cite|news|last=Mikkelson |first=Barbara |date=May 18, 2010 |title=Golf: Gentlemen Only, Ladies Forbidden? |work=[[Snopes]] |url=https://www.snopes.com/fact-check/golf-carte-2/ |access-date=June 23, 2022 |archive-date=June 23, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220623214025/https://www.snopes.com/fact-check/golf-carte-2/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="usga">{{#invoke:Cite|news|last=Jerris |first=Rand |title=FAQ – Golf History Questions |work=[[United States Golf Association]] |url=https://www.usga.org/history/faq--golf-history-questions-232994f0.html |access-date=June 23, 2022 |archive-date=September 4, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220904025029/https://www.usga.org/history/faq--golf-history-questions-232994f0.html |url-status=live }}</ref> It may have come from the Dutch word ''kolf'' or ''kolve'', meaning "club",<ref name="usga" /> or from the Scottish word ''goulf'' or ''gowf'' meaning "to strike or cuff".<ref name="golfsnopes" />
* [[Origins of baseball|Baseball was not invented]] by [[Doubleday myth|Abner Doubleday]], nor did it originate in [[Cooperstown, New York]]. It is believed to have evolved from the [[bat-and-ball game]] [[rounders]] and first [[Knickerbocker Rules|took its modern form]] in New York City.<ref>a. {{cite magazine |last=Cole |first=Diane |url=https://www.usnews.com/usnews/news/articles/060806/14ball.htm |title=Contrary to myth, baseball may have had no single inventor |magazine=U.S. News & World Report |date=October 4, 1990 |access-date=August 6, 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131214105928/http://www.usnews.com/usnews/news/articles/060806/14ball.htm |archive-date=December 14, 2013}}
<br />b. {{#invoke:Cite|news|last=Fox|first=Butterfield|url=https://www.nytimes.com/1990/10/04/nyregion/cooperstown-hoboken-try-new-york-city.html|title=Cooperstown? Hoboken? Try New York City|work=The New York Times|date=October 4, 1990|access-date=April 3, 2009|archive-date=January 9, 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110109170603/http://www.nytimes.com/1990/10/04/nyregion/cooperstown-hoboken-try-new-york-city.html|url-status=live}}</ref>
[[File:Marcos black belt.jpg|thumb|150px|A [[BJJ belt|BJJ black belt]] with a red bar indicating first degree]]
* The [[black belt (martial arts)|black belt in martial arts]] does not necessarily indicate expert level.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=VanBlaricum |first=Michael |date=August 29, 2010 |title=Black Belt Realities |url=https://www.usadojo.com/black-belt-realities/ |access-date=June 24, 2024 |website=USADojo.com}}</ref> It was introduced for [[judo]] in the 1880s to indicate competency at all of the basic techniques of the sport. Promotion beyond 1st [[dan (rank)|dan]] (the first black belt rank) varies among different martial arts.<ref>{{Cite web |title=[スポーツ] 柔道帯の最高位は、何と紅!?“紅帯”所持者に投げられてきた! {{!}} RxR {{!}} R25.jp |url=http://r25.jp/magazine/ranking_review/10002000/1112008051512.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081004010127/http://r25.jp/magazine/ranking_review/10002000/1112008051512.html |archive-date=2008-10-04 |access-date=2026-02-03 |website=r25.jp |language=ja}}</ref>
* The use of [[triangular corner flags in English football]] is not a privilege reserved for those teams that have won an [[FA Cup]] in the past, as depicted in a scene in the film ''[[Twin Town]]''. The [[Football Association]]'s rules are silent on the subject, and the decision over what shape flag to use has been up to the individual club's groundskeepers.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|first=Jack |last=Williams |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/02/17/sports/soccer/corner-flags-english-premier-league-champion.html |title=You Can Tell an F.A. Cup Champion by Its Corner Flags. Or Not. |newspaper=The New York Times |date=February 17, 2017 |access-date=April 8, 2018 |archive-date=January 25, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200125100113/https://www.nytimes.com/2017/02/17/sports/soccer/corner-flags-english-premier-league-champion.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
* [[India men's national football team|India]] did not withdraw from the [[1950 FIFA World Cup]] because their squad wanted to play barefoot. In reality, India withdrew because the country's managing body, the [[All India Football Federation]] (AIFF), was insufficiently prepared for the team's participation.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last1=Adhikari |first1=Somak |title=No, India Did Not Withdraw From The 1950 FIFA World Cup Because They Did Not Want To Wear Shoes |url=https://www.indiatimes.com/sports/kick-off/why-did-india-withdraw-from-the-1950-world-cup-no-it-wasn-t-because-they-had-to-wear-shoes-258199.html |website=The Times of India |publisher=The Times Group |access-date=December 2, 2019 |date=June 6, 2018 |archive-date=January 25, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200125100007/https://www.indiatimes.com/sports/kick-off/why-did-india-withdraw-from-the-1950-world-cup-no-it-wasn-t-because-they-had-to-wear-shoes-258199.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

== Video games ==
* There is no definitive proof that [[Violence and video games#Scientific debate|violent video games cause people to become violent]]. Some studies have found no link between aggression and violent video games,<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|date=February 13, 2019 |title=Violent video games found not to be associated with adolescent aggression |url=https://www.ox.ac.uk/news/2019-02-13-violent-video-games-found-not-be-associated-adolescent-aggression |access-date=June 25, 2022 |website=[[University of Oxford]] |language=en |archive-date=June 25, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220625163510/https://www.ox.ac.uk/news/2019-02-13-violent-video-games-found-not-be-associated-adolescent-aggression |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{#invoke:Cite|news|last1=Etchells |first1=Pete |date=April 6, 2019 |title=Five damaging myths about video games – let's shoot 'em up |work=[[The Guardian]] |url=https://www.theguardian.com/games/2019/apr/06/five-damaging-myths-about-video-games-lets-shoot-em-up |access-date=December 23, 2020}}</ref> and the popularity of gaming has coincided with a ''decrease'' in youth violence.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Henry |first=Jenkins |title=Reality Bytes: Eight Myths About Video Games Debunked |url=https://www.pbs.org/kcts/videogamerevolution/impact/myths.html |access-date=December 23, 2020 |website=[[PBS]] |archive-date=August 26, 2004 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040826014153/http://www.pbs.org/kcts/videogamerevolution/impact/myths.html |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Misconceptions – Video Game Dissection |url=https://sites.psu.edu/videogamebreakdown/stereotypes/ |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20221022235359/https://sites.psu.edu/videogamebreakdown/stereotypes/ |archive-date=2022-10-22 |access-date=2026-02-03 |website=sites.psu.edu |language=en-US}}</ref> The [[moral panic]] surrounding video games in the 1980s through to the 2020s, alongside several studies and incidents of violence and legislation in many countries, likely contributed to proliferating this idea.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|journal|last1=Markey |first1=Patrick M. |last2=Ferguson |first2=Christopher J. |date=October 1, 2017 |title=Teaching Us to Fear: The Violent Video Game Moral Panic and the Politics of Game Research |url=https://www.journalofplay.org/sites/www.journalofplay.org/files/pdf-articles/10-1-Article-4-Teaching-us-to-fear.pdf |journal=[[American Journal of Play]] |pages=99–115 |access-date=December 23, 2020 |archive-date=June 29, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210629065109/https://www.journalofplay.org/sites/www.journalofplay.org/files/pdf-articles/10-1-Article-4-Teaching-us-to-fear.pdf }}</ref><ref name="NYTimes 20190805">{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Draper |first=Kevin |date=August 5, 2019 |title=Video Games Aren't Why Shootings Happen. Politicians Still Blame Them. |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2019/08/05/sports/trump-violent-video-games-studies.html |access-date=August 22, 2019 |work=[[The New York Times]] |archive-date=April 26, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200426031749/https://www.nytimes.com/2019/08/05/sports/trump-violent-video-games-studies.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
* The so-called "[[Nuclear Gandhi]]" glitch, in which peaceful leader [[Mahatma Gandhi]] would become unusually aggressive if [[democracy]] was adopted,<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Plunkett |first=Luke |title=Why Gandhi Is Such An Asshole In Civilization |url=https://kotaku.com/why-gandhi-is-such-an-asshole-in-civilization-1653818245 |website=[[Kotaku]] |date=February 3, 2016 |access-date=June 22, 2022 |language=en |archive-date=September 18, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200918192554/https://kotaku.com/why-gandhi-is-such-an-asshole-in-civilization-1653818245 |url-status=live }}</ref> did not exist in either the original ''[[Civilization (video game)|Civilization]]'' game or ''[[Civilization II]]''. The games' designer [[Sid Meier]] attributed the origins of the rumor to both a [[TV Tropes]] thread and a [[Know Your Meme]] entry,<ref name="memoir!">{{#invoke:Cite|book| author = [[Sid Meier|Meier, Sid]] | chapter = Funny Business| chapter-url = | url = | title = Sid Meier's Memoir!: A Life in Computer Games|date = 2020 |publisher= [[W. W. Norton & Company|W. W. Norton]] | pages = 261–266| isbn = 978-1-324-00587-2}}</ref> while [[Reddit]] and a ''[[Kotaku]]'' article helped popularize it.<ref name="dtf probably myth">{{#invoke:Cite|web|url=https://dtf.ru/games/67979-pochemu-istoriya-o-bage-s-yadernym-gandi-v-civilization-skoree-vsego-vydumana |script-title=ru:Почему история о баге с «ядерным Ганди» в Civilization, скорее всего, выдумана |trans-title=Why the story about the "Nuclear Gandhi" bug in Civilization is likely fictional |author=Артемий Леонов |website={{ill|DTF (website)|lt=DTF.ru|ru|DTF}} |date=September 5, 2019 |language=ru |access-date=June 22, 2022 |archive-date=September 26, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200926050823/https://dtf.ru/games/67979-pochemu-istoriya-o-bage-s-yadernym-gandi-v-civilization-skoree-vsego-vydumana |url-status=live }}</ref> Gandhi's supposed behavior did appear in the 2010 ''[[Civilization V]]''<ref name="memoir!" /> as a joke, and in 2016's ''[[Civilization VI|VI]]''<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|last=Jackson |first=Gina |url=https://kotaku.com/gandhi-is-still-an-asshole-in-civilization-vi-1788710568 |title=Gandhi Is Still An Asshole In Civilization VI |website=[[Kotaku]] |date=August 11, 2016 |access-date=June 22, 2022 |language=en |archive-date=September 18, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200918192556/https://kotaku.com/gandhi-is-still-an-asshole-in-civilization-vi-1788710568 |url-status=live }}</ref> as a reference to the legend.
* The Japanese government did not pass a law banning [[Square Enix]] from releasing the ''[[Dragon Quest]]'' games on weekdays due to it causing too many schoolchildren to cut class. This rule is self-imposed by the developers.<ref>{{#invoke:Cite|web|first=Skrebels|last=Joe|date=April 22, 2020|title=How Dragon Quest Spawned an Urban Legend|url=https://www.ign.com/articles/how-dragon-quest-spawned-an-urban-myth|website=[[IGN]]|access-date=June 26, 2022|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220414195652/https://www.ign.com/articles/how-dragon-quest-spawned-an-urban-myth|archive-date=April 14, 2022}}</ref>
* The release of ''[[Space Invaders]]'' in 1978 did not cause a shortage of [[100 yen coin|¥100 coins]] in [[Japan]]. An advertising campaign by [[Taito]] and an erroneous 1980 article in ''[[New Scientist]]'' are the sources of this claim.<ref name="Numismatist">{{#invoke:Cite|journal|last=Paradis|first=Charles|date=March 2014|title=Insert Coin to Play: Space Invaders and the 100-Yen Myth|journal=[[The Numismatist]]|pages=46–48|publisher=[[American Numismatic Association]]|url=https://www.academia.edu/3672374|access-date=June 26, 2022|archive-date=October 21, 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221021083122/https://www.academia.edu/3672374|url-status=live}}</ref>

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

[[Category:Lists of common misconceptions|Arts and culture]]
[[Category:Arts-related lists|common misconceptions]]
